lessons.applies_when: There exists a privileged write to bypass/privilege state (excludedFromFees/whitelist/limitsExempt/blacklist/etc.) that is onlyOwner/onlyAdmin, but another public/external function with a different/weaker gate calls it (or performs the same write) passing an address parameter influenced by that function’s inputs, without restricting that address to msg.sender or a fixed/validated set.

lessons.lesson: Flag owner-gated installation of an arbitrary external module/guard address followed by an external call into it, especially when the call grants it authority (setOwner/setAdmin/setOperator). This creates an indirect authorization channel if the module is unconstrained.
lessons.applies_when: An onlyOwner/onlyAdmin function sets a module/guard/hook/antiBot address from a parameter without visible trust constraints (whitelist/immutable/codehash/factory check) and, within the same function/transaction, calls into that newly-set module (initialize/configure), including calls that assign authority in the module (setTokenOwner/transferOwnership/setAdmin/setOperator/etc.).

lessons.lesson: Flag cross-contract privilege reassignment when an owner/admin-gated function calls an external module’s owner/controller setter and passes a caller-derived or parameter-derived principal as the new owner/controller; local onlyOwner gating does not prevent mis-assignment of the external authorization root.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function is gated by onlyOwner/admin/role and makes an external call like module.set*Owner/transferOwnership/setAdmin/setController/setOperator, where the new owner/controller argument is msg.sender/_msgSender() or otherwise derived from the caller or function parameters (not a fixed constant like address(this) or a stored, validated owner address).

lessons.lesson: Flag permissionless external/public trigger functions that unconditionally call a privileged-configurable external dependency (module/reflector/hook/router), because they expose a user-triggerable entrypoint into owner-selected code.
lessons.applies_when: An onlyOwner/onlyAdmin function assigns an external contract address used later for calls without tight trust constraints, AND a public/external function lacking caller-identity gating performs an unconditional external call to that address with no additional authorization or selector filtering.

lessons.lesson: Do not treat downstream onlyOwner as protective if an unguarded public/external wrapper calls it across a contract boundary where msg.sender becomes the wrapper contract; this creates an alternative path that lets any user trigger an owner-protected sensitive action indirectly.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function F in contract A has no caller restriction AND F calls contract B where the called function is guarded by onlyOwner/require(msg.sender==owner) AND B’s owner is (or can be set to) contract A AND the called function performs a sensitive effect (value transfer/claim/withdrawal/finalization or accounting that results in payouts).

lessons.lesson: Do not treat a generic owner-mutable authorized/operators allowlist as sufficient access control for high-impact knobs; if non-owner allowlisted accounts can change payout routing or trigger distributions/swaps of contract-held funds, that is an improper access boundary.
lessons.applies_when: There exists an authorized/operators mapping used in a modifier/check AND an onlyOwner/admin function can add/remove arbitrary addresses to it AND non-owner allowlisted functions can (a) set payout/receiver/treasury addresses or fee/tax parameters, or (b) trigger transfers/swaps/distributions of ETH/ERC20 from address(this).

lessons.lesson: Flag owner/role-gated blacklist/transfer-restriction controls as CWE-284 when they can target arbitrary third-party accounts and are enforced in core transfer paths; the protected resource is users’ ability to transact, so overbroad restriction scope is itself an access-control weakness.
lessons.applies_when: An onlyOwner/admin/role function can set per-address restriction mappings/flags or global enforcement toggles for caller-supplied target addresses (blacklist, cooldown, fee/limit exemptions, tx/wallet limits, market-pair flags, etc.), AND transfer execution reads those flags/toggles to deny or materially constrain transfers (revert, cooldown blocks, maxTx/maxWallet, punitive fees), with no tight constraints on which addresses can be targeted (no hardcoded allowlist, one-way/immutable set, or verified-factory pair/router constraint).

lessons.lesson: Treat owner-gated router/pair replacement as access-control relevant when those addresses are used as privileged exceptions or gatekeepers in transfer restrictions (e.g., AMM pair/router exemptions).
lessons.applies_when: An onlyOwner/admin function can set/replace DEX-critical addresses (router, pair, AMM pair mapping) from parameters, AND transfer/trading permission logic depends on those addresses to allow/deny or exempt behavior.

lessons.lesson: Flag permissionless deposit/funding/reward functions as access-control issues when they mutate contract-global (non-user-keyed) distribution/fee accumulators, especially when the update uses caller-controlled values rather than a verified received-token delta; shared accounting is a security-sensitive resource even if tokens are transferred from msg.sender.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function lacks any caller-identity/role gate AND it writes (assignment or +=/-=) to a contract-wide accumulator not keyed by msg.sender (rewardsFeeTotal/totalRewards/accRewardPerShare/rewardIndex/cumulative*/feeTotal*), particularly when the increment is derived from an input parameter/return value rather than computed from balanceAfter-balanceBefore (or other verifiable receipt) in that function.

lessons.lesson: Treat hard-coded nonzero authority assignment (owner/admin/DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) as an access-control failure when that role gates sensitive operations; consistent onlyOwner usage does not negate that the authorization root is improperly pre-granted to an arbitrary principal.
lessons.applies_when: In a constructor/initializer, an authority variable used by onlyOwner/onlyAdmin/onlyRole checks is assigned an address literal 0x... (or constant address), rather than msg.sender/_msgSender() or a validated parameter, AND at least one public/external function gated solely by that authority performs security-sensitive effects (fees/limits/blacklist/exemptions/router/pair updates; withdrawals/rescues; transfers/swaps from address(this)).

lessons.lesson: Treat missing visibility into inherited/modifier/identity-helper code or omitted bodies on privileged paths as an unresolved alternate-entry-point risk; treat authorization as unverified rather than proven safe.
lessons.applies_when: Any of: (1) inheritance/imported parents not visible and public/external state-changing entry points may be inherited; (2) a security-sensitive function relies on a modifier or identity/authorization helper whose definition is not present; (3) ellipses/omitted regions exist on the path to privileged effects; or (4) a privileged sink is reached via a call to a function whose body is not visible (unseen parent/abstract/forward-declared).

lessons.lesson: Do not return a definitive not_vulnerable verdict for CWE-284 from tests-only or partial-entry-point snippets; if you cannot enumerate all public/external entry points that reach privileged state changes or value-movement sinks, treat access control as unverified/at-risk.
lessons.applies_when: The visible subset is only tests; OR shows only private/internal helpers without the public/external callers; OR the snippet is incomplete (missing files/parents/imports, ellipses, abstract/unseen function bodies) while also containing privileged effects/sinks (role/owner/exemption/config writes; router/pair/treasury setters; ETH/ERC20 transfer/swap from address(this)).

lessons.lesson: Do not assume transfer/transferFrom/burn enforcement when restrictions are expressed only in an internal token hook/override; if the external entrypoints that should invoke the hook are not visible, treat the restriction as potentially bypassable via inherited/public entrypoints.
lessons.applies_when: Policy enforcement is located only inside an internal hook/override (_beforeTokenTransfer/_update/_transfer override) but the inherited concrete external transfer entrypoints are not visible (missing imports/parents/ellipses), and the code otherwise exposes restriction state/mappings suggesting intended enforcement.

lessons.lesson: Do not treat “private sensitive function is only called by an onlyOwner function” as proof of safety when the inherited external surface is not shown; missing parent code can create alternate external paths into the same sensitive sink.
lessons.applies_when: The snippet shows an internal/private sensitive sink (swap/fee distribution/treasury send/rescue/withdraw) AND imports/parents or standard external entrypoints are not shown, AND the not_vulnerable rationale relies on onlyOwner reachability within the subset.

lessons.lesson: Do not conclude “no bypass/unprotected entry point exists” when the snippet is partial but already shows multiple onlyOwner/onlyRole privileged knobs; treat the privileged surface as unenumerated and keep CWE-284 as at-risk unless all public/external state-changing entrypoints are visible.
lessons.applies_when: The code is an excerpt/partial view (missing files/parents/imports/ellipses or referenced-but-not-shown functions/modifiers) AND the visible portion shows at least one onlyOwner/onlyRole/admin-gated setter/toggle for security-sensitive configuration.

lessons.lesson: Treat a hook override that omits the required `super` call as a concrete access-control bypass condition when enforcement is expected to be composed via parent hook chaining (common in token pause/blacklist/limit designs).
lessons.applies_when: A contract inherits from one or more base token contracts (ERC20/BEP20 variants) AND overrides a security-relevant hook such as `_beforeTokenTransfer`, `_update`, `_transfer`, or `_afterTokenTransfer` AND the overriding function body does not call `super.<hook>(...)` on all paths (no unconditional `super` call) AND the inherited base implementations that could contain enforcement logic are not visible in the snippet (imports/parents omitted).

lessons.lesson: Do not clear CWE-284 based on “all visible privileged functions are onlyOwner/onlyRole” when the contract is explicitly truncated; treat incomplete source markers as preventing a global claim that no unauthorized entry point exists.
lessons.applies_when: The provided Solidity snippet contains explicit truncation/incompleteness indicators (e.g., "...", "omitted", "rest of contract", unfinished contract body) AND the visible portion includes at least one security-sensitive sink (ETH/ERC20 transfer from address(this), rescue/withdraw, role/owner/admin updates, router/pair/treasury/payout setters, pause/trading/limit toggles, blacklist/whitelist/exemption writes).

lessons.lesson: Treat ownership/role restoration or reassignment functions as a bypass if they authorize with a different identity primitive than the contract’s primary access-control guard (e.g., onlyOwner uses _msgSender() but restore/unlock uses msg.sender), because the authorized principal can diverge under Context/forwarder patterns.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract: (1) an access-control modifier or check for privileged actions uses `_msgSender()`/`Context` (e.g., `require(_owner == _msgSender())`), AND (2) a public/external function that sets/recovers owner/admin/role state (e.g., `_owner = ...`, `transferOwnership`, `unlock`, `restoreOwnership`) is not protected by that modifier and instead authorizes with `msg.sender` (e.g., `require(_previousOwner == msg.sender)`), or otherwise uses a different caller-identity resolver than the primary guard.

lessons.lesson: Treat opaque/meta-tx “context resolver” identities as attacker-controlled unless the function itself enforces a binding between the resolved address and the immediate caller (or a shown trusted-forwarder check). If a sensitive state write is keyed by `resolvedAddress = _getCurrentContextAddress()` (or similar) and there is no explicit constraint that `resolvedAddress` equals `msg.sender/_msgSender()` (or that `msg.sender` is a verified forwarder acting for `resolvedAddress`), flag CWE-284.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external state-changing function computes an address principal via an unshown/opaque resolver (e.g., `_getCurrentContextAddress()`, `getContextAddress()`, `context.msgSender()`, `trustedForwarderContext()`) and then writes security-relevant state under that principal as a mapping key (e.g., `mapping[address=>...]` or `mapping[address=>mapping[address=>...]]`), AND within the same function there is no `require(resolved == msg.sender/_msgSender())`, no `require(isTrustedForwarder(msg.sender))` + signature/appendix validation shown, and no per-user approval mapping tying `msg.sender` to act for `resolved`.

lessons.lesson: Treat “anyone can call + signature from a stored signer address” as an access-control boundary for privileged sinks (mint/withdraw/admin writes), and flag CWE-284 when the signer key is an on-chain mutable authority root rather than a fixed, externally-auditable trust anchor.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function (e.g., claim/redeem/execute) reaches a privileged sink such as `_mint(...)`, balance/totalSupply mutations, or ETH/ERC20 transfers from `address(this)`, and the only authorization check is `ecrecover`/`ECDSA.recover` against a state variable like `signer`, `signerAddress`, `validator`, or `authorizer` (no `onlyOwner`/`onlyRole`/per-user allowance gate on the caller), AND there exists any on-chain setter that can change that signer variable (e.g., `setSigner`, `updateSigner`, `signerAddressUpdate`) guarded only by owner/admin/role or otherwise.

lessons.lesson: Treat constructor/initializer transfers of the authorization root to a hard-coded nonzero EOA/address literal as an access-control failure when that root gates privileged operations; consistent onlyOwner checks do not negate that the privileged principal is pre-selected in code.
lessons.applies_when: In a constructor/initializer: (a) `owner/admin` (or DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE holder) is set via `transferOwnership(<address literal>)`, direct assignment `owner = <address literal>`, or `_grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, <address literal>)`, where `<address literal>` is a nonzero hard-coded constant; AND (b) at least one public/external function guarded by that authority performs a security-sensitive effect (fund/token rescue/withdraw, payout/treasury routing, fee/limit/blacklist/whitelist/exemption updates, router/pair/module updates, pausing/trading toggles, mint/burn/balance/supply mutation).

lessons.lesson: Treat non-owner “authorized/operator” roles as an access-control failure when they can edit protocol-wide enforcement or exemptions for arbitrary third-party addresses (not just themselves). This is a scope problem: the protected resource is the integrity of global restrictions (fees/limits/trading/anti-bot), so granting that capability to a broad allowlist is improper access control even though a modifier exists.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function is gated by a non-owner allowlist modifier/check (e.g., `authorized[msg.sender]`, `isOperator[msg.sender]`, `onlyAuthorized`) AND the function (a) writes to exemption/privilege mappings or policy knobs used in core transfer/trading restriction logic (e.g., `isExcludedFromFees`, `isTxLimitExempt`, `isWalletLimitExempt`, blacklist/whitelist, fee multipliers, maxTx/maxWallet, tradingEnabled/limits toggles) AND (b) accepts caller-supplied target address(es) via parameters (single address or `address[] calldata`) and updates those targets without `require(target == msg.sender)` or an owner-only gate.

lessons.lesson: Treat admin-mutable bypass allowlists inside transfer/trading gates as access-control resources: even if the setter is onlyOwner, the contract is CWE-284-relevant when the owner can arbitrarily grant selected third-party addresses an exemption that bypasses a protocol-wide restriction (e.g., tradingClosed/paused/limits) enforced in the core transfer path.
lessons.applies_when: In the token transfer path (`transfer`/`transferFrom`/`_transfer`/`_update`): a global restriction is enforced (e.g., `require(tradingOpen)` / `require(!paused)` / maxTx/maxWallet) AND that restriction is conditionally skipped when `from`/`to`/`msg.sender` matches a mutable mapping/flag (e.g., `isExcluded[...]`, `isDexPair[...]`, `isRouter[...]`, `isWhitelisted[...]`) AND there exists any public/external admin function (often onlyOwner) that can set/unset that mapping/flag for arbitrary caller-supplied addresses (single `address` or `address[]`) without tight constraints (no hardcoded set, no factory/verified-pair check, no one-way immutability).

lessons.lesson: Treat any public/external owner/admin reassignment (including time-lock “unlock/restore” patterns) that writes the authorization root without using the contract’s primary access-control guard as a CWE-284 trigger, when it relies on an alternate stored authority identity (e.g., previousOwner) rather than the current owner/role system.
lessons.applies_when: A contract defines a primary access-control primitive for privileged actions (e.g., `onlyOwner`, `onlyRole`, or `require(msg.sender == owner/_owner/admin)`) AND there exists a public/external function that directly assigns the authority variable (`owner/_owner/admin = ...` or calls an internal setter like `_transferOwnership(...)`) WITHOUT that primary modifier/check, and instead gates via a different stored identity variable or predicate (e.g., `require(msg.sender == _previousOwner)` / `require(msg.sender == pendingOwner)` / `require(msg.sender == locker)`), optionally plus time/flag conditions.

lessons.lesson: Flag bypassable global transfer/trading gates when the core enforcement `require(...)` is wrapped in an exemption predicate keyed by an admin-mutable per-address mapping (sender/recipient), because this creates a selective access-control bypass in the primary execution path even if all admin setters are `onlyOwner`.
lessons.applies_when: In `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`_transfer`/`_update` (or equivalent core path) there is a global restriction like `require(tradingOpen)` / `require(!paused)` / maxTx/maxWallet enforced only under a condition such as `if (!isExcluded[from] && !isExcluded[to]) { require(restriction); }` (or any logic that skips the restriction when `isExcluded[from]` OR `isExcluded[to]` is true), AND there exists a public/external admin function that can set/unset that exemption mapping/flag for arbitrary addresses (takes `address` or `address[]` parameter; not restricted to `msg.sender`).

lessons.lesson: Flag as CWE-284 when role-gated privileged operations are effectively broadened at deployment by granting the same role to an extra default principal (commonly msg.sender/deployer) in addition to an apparent intended fixed management/admin address.
lessons.applies_when: In a constructor/initializer, the contract both (a) assigns a specific non-parameter authority address (hard-coded literal or named constant like `MANAGEMENT_WALLET`) as a role holder/admin (e.g., `_setupRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, MANAGEMENT_WALLET)` / `_grantRole(MANAGEMENT_ROLE, MANAGEMENT_WALLET)`), AND (b) also grants that same privileged role (or the gate role used by `onlyManager`/`onlyRole(MANAGEMENT_ROLE)`) to `msg.sender` via `_setupRole(...)`, `_grantRole(...)`, or equivalent, AND at least one public/external state-changing function is gated only by that role.

lessons.lesson: Treat owner-to-zero renounce paths as an access-control failure when owner is the sole authorization root for post-deployment operational controls: bricking critical onlyOwner actions is an access-control breakdown even without an unauthorized caller bypass.
lessons.applies_when: Contract is Ownable-like with `renounceOwnership()` or any public/external owner setter that can make `owner/_owner/admin == address(0)` (no `require(newOwner != address(0))`), AND there exists at least one other public/external function guarded by `onlyOwner`/`require(msg.sender==owner)` that is an ongoing operational control (e.g., trading/paused toggles, router/pair/market setters, emergency withdraw/rescue, config that must be adjustable post-deploy), AND no alternative non-zero admin/role path is visible.

lessons.lesson: Treat onlyOwner/onlyRole checks that rely on an unseen identity indirection (e.g., `_msgSender()`/Context) as unverified: if the resolver implementation is not present, you cannot conclude the access check is correct and should keep CWE-284 as at-risk.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function reaches a security-sensitive sink (e.g., `_mint`, balance/totalSupply mutation, withdrawals/transfers, privileged config writes) and is gated by a modifier/require that compares against `_msgSender()` (directly or via `onlyOwner`/`onlyRole`), AND the definition of `_msgSender()` / `Context` (or equivalent identity-resolver base) is not included in the provided code (missing import/parent/body).

lessons.lesson: Treat deployment-time (constructor/initializer) ETH/ERC20 transfers to constructor-supplied recipients as access-control surfaces: if the recipient/amount are externally parameterized and not restricted to a trusted principal, the contract is vulnerable even if all post-deployment privileged functions are onlyOwner.
lessons.applies_when: Inside a constructor/initializer: (1) there is a value-movement sink such as `recipient.transfer(x)`, `recipient.call{value:x}(...)`, `IERC20(token).transfer(recipient,x)`, or `SafeERC20.safeTransfer(token, recipient, x)`; AND (2) `recipient` and/or `x` is derived from a constructor/initializer parameter (or from a struct/array parameter) rather than a hard-coded constant or `msg.sender`; AND (3) there is no explicit restriction in the constructor/initializer that binds the recipient/amount to a privileged/expected value (e.g., `require(recipient == owner/admin/treasury)` or `require(recipient != address(0))` plus a whitelist/immutable constant, and `require(x == expectedFee)` or `require(x <= cap)` tied to verifiable inputs).

lessons.lesson: Treat owner-renounce-to-dead-address functions as an access-control failure when they can permanently make the contract’s sole authorization predicate unsatisfiable, even if the renounce function itself is onlyOwner.
lessons.applies_when: Contract uses an owner-rooted guard (e.g., `onlyOwner` / `require(_owner == _msgSender())`) for one or more security-sensitive admin operations AND there exists a public/external function callable by the current owner (often `onlyOwner`) that assigns the owner variable to `address(0)` OR a hard-coded “dead/burn” literal (e.g., `0x000000000000000000000000000000000000dEaD`) AND there is no visible recovery path (no `transferOwnership`/`acceptOwnership`/`restoreOwner` callable without the same now-unsatisfiable guard).

lessons.lesson: Treat irreversible owner-nullification (renounce/transfer-to-zero/dead) as an access-control failure when that owner variable is the sole authorization root for any post-deployment security-sensitive function, because it makes authorization permanently unsatisfiable (privileged actions become unreachable).
lessons.applies_when: All of the following are statically visible: (1) an Ownable-style guard exists (e.g., `onlyOwner` or `require(msg.sender == owner/_owner)` / `_checkOwner()`), (2) a public/external function callable by the current owner assigns the authorization-root variable to `address(0)` or a hard-coded burn/dead literal (e.g., `renounceOwnership(){ _owner = address(0); }` or `transferOwnership(address(0))` without `require(newOwner != address(0))`), (3) at least one other public/external function with security-sensitive effects is gated solely by that owner variable, and (4) there is no visible recovery path that can restore a nonzero owner without requiring the same now-unsatisfiable owner check (no 2-step `pendingOwner` acceptance callable by the new owner, no alternate admin role).

lessons.lesson: Treat `onlyOwner`-gated owner-nullification to an unreachable nonzero “dead/burn” literal as an access-control failure when `owner` is the sole authorization root for other ongoing privileged operations; this is not merely availability—authorization becomes permanently unsatisfiable for all protected actions.
lessons.applies_when: All are visible in source: (1) an `onlyOwner`-style guard exists keyed solely to an `owner/_owner/admin` address; (2) a public/external function callable by the current owner (e.g., `waiveOwnership`, `renounceOwnership`, `transferOwnership`) assigns that variable to a hard-coded nonzero burn/dead/unreachable address literal (not `msg.sender` or a validated parameter), e.g., `0x000...dEaD`; (3) at least one other public/external security-sensitive function is gated solely by that same owner variable; and (4) no visible recovery path can restore a valid owner without passing the same now-unsatisfiable owner check (no `pendingOwner` acceptance, no alternate admin role).

lessons.lesson: Treat explicit truncation around the constructor/initializer as an access-control unknown: if ownership/role initialization is not fully visible, do not clear CWE-284 just because visible admin functions are onlyOwner.
lessons.applies_when: The provided snippet contains explicit incompleteness markers ("...", "// ...", "omitted", unfinished contract body) within or around the constructor/initializer (or initializer-like function), AND the contract includes at least one onlyOwner/onlyRole gated security-sensitive sink (e.g., fee/limit/exemption mapping writes, router/pair/module setters, pause/trading toggles, withdrawals/rescues, mint/burn/balance/supply mutation).

lessons.lesson: Treat references to non-shown external entry points or internal helpers that plausibly reach sensitive writes as blocking a "no bypass" conclusion: consistent onlyOwner on visible setters is not sufficient if other reachable paths are missing.
lessons.applies_when: A snippet shows onlyOwner/onlyRole gated sensitive state writes, AND the same contract references but does not include the bodies of (a) core external entry points (e.g., transfer/transferFrom/claim/withdraw/execute) or (b) internal helpers likely invoked from such entry points (e.g., _tokenTransfer/_transfer/_update/restoreAllFee/swapBack/distribute), evidenced by function calls to undefined symbols, forward declarations, inheritance with missing parent bodies, or comments indicating omitted sections.

lessons.lesson: Flag principal/resource mismatches introduced at deployment: if the constructor/initializer allocates the core asset/resource (e.g., entire token supply) to one externally-specified principal but sets the contract’s access-control root (owner/admin) to a different principal, treat it as improper access control even when all privileged setters are `onlyOwner`.
lessons.applies_when: In a constructor/initializer, BOTH are true: (1) a resource-grant is made to an address parameter/variable not equal to `msg.sender/_msgSender()` (e.g., `_mint(tokenOwner, totalSupply)`, `_balances[tokenOwner]=...`, `_rOwned[tokenOwner]=_rTotal`, or equivalent full/majority initial allocation), AND (2) ownership/admin is set to `msg.sender/_msgSender()` (e.g., `_transferOwnership(_msgSender())`, `owner = _msgSender()`, `_grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, _msgSender())`) with NO in-constructor binding that equates them (`require(tokenOwner == _msgSender())`) and NO immediate handoff (`transferOwnership(tokenOwner)` / `_transferOwnership(tokenOwner)` / `_grantRole(..., tokenOwner)` as the actual gate root).

lessons.lesson: Do not clear CWE-284 when the writeup’s safety argument depends on “no other public/external entry point exists” but the code is missing any portion that could define entry points (inherited ERC20 surface, omitted sections, or referenced-but-unseen functions). Treat access control as unverified/at-risk in that case.
lessons.applies_when: The provided source is a subset (evidence: missing parent contracts/imports that define external entrypoints like ERC20, explicit truncation markers such as "..."/"omitted", or calls to functions/modifiers whose bodies are not shown) AND the non-vulnerable rationale relies on the absence of alternative public/external entry points to reach a sensitive sink (privileged state writes, blacklist/whitelist/exemption mapping writes, admin config, or value transfer) rather than demonstrating complete enumeration of externally callable paths.

lessons.lesson: Do not issue a global "no unauthorized external path" conclusion for CWE-284 when any security-critical routine on the privileged/value-moving path is explicitly truncated; treat access control as unverified (at-risk) because omitted lines can contain alternate gates, bypass branches, or external entry points that reach the same sink.
lessons.applies_when: The provided snippet contains explicit truncation markers ("...", "// ...", "omitted") inside or immediately around (a) a core externally-reachable execution path (e.g., `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`_transfer`, claim/withdraw, fallback) OR (b) a sensitive sink routine (e.g., swap/distribution/withdrawal/rescue or privileged config write), and the verdict rationale depends on the absence of other public/external entry points or bypasses.

lessons.lesson: Flag blocklist enforcement as incomplete when ERC20 `transferFrom(from, to, amount)` gates only on the caller (`msg.sender`) being unblocked but does not gate on the debited account (`from`) being unblocked; this defeats the intended restriction on blocked accounts via third-party spenders with allowance.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains (1) a blocklist mapping like `mapping(address=>bool) _blocked/blocked`; (2) a helper/modifier used in transfer paths that checks only `blocked[msg.sender]` (e.g., `function isBlocked() returns blocked[msg.sender]` or `require(!blocked[_msgSender()])`); AND (3) `transferFrom(address from, address to, uint256 amount)` (or internal `_spendAllowance(from, msg.sender, ...)` + `_transfer(from,to,...)` path) lacks any `require(!blocked[from])`/`require(!blocked[_from])` check (directly or via a modifier that references `from`).

lessons.lesson: Flag any alternate public/external token-movement entry point that bypasses a global trading/allowlist gate applied to the standard ERC20 surface (transfer/transferFrom/approve); bypassing intended market-open restrictions is itself an access-control failure even when tokens are debited only from msg.sender.
lessons.applies_when: All of the following are visible: (1) a global transfer/trading gate exists in a modifier/helper used by standard ERC20 entrypoints, e.g., `require(tradingEnabled || allowedTransfer[<principal>])` applied to `transfer`, `transferFrom`, and/or `approve`; AND (2) there is at least one other `public`/`external` function that performs token movement via `_transfer(...)` / direct balance updates / internal transfer helper (including `_transfer(msg.sender, ...)`), but that function does NOT apply the same gate/modifier and does not replicate the same `require(...)` condition; AND (3) the function is callable by arbitrary users (no onlyOwner/onlyRole restriction).

lessons.lesson: Treat "approve-and-call" (or ERC1363/677-style) patterns as access-control surfaces: a public/external function that performs an external call to a caller-supplied contract address (callee) is a privileged capability unless the callee is restricted (whitelist/role/immutable trusted contract).
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function (often named `approveAndCall`, `approveAndCallcode`, `transferAndCall`, `approveAndCallWithSender`) takes an address parameter (e.g., `_spender`, `_to`, `receiver`) and then invokes an external call on that address (e.g., `tokenRecipient(_spender).receiveApproval(...)`, `IERC1363Receiver(receiver).onApprovalReceived(...)`, `IERC677Receiver(to).onTokenTransfer(...)`, or low-level `.call(...)`) where the target address is derived directly from the parameter and there is no visible restriction such as `require(whitelist[target])`, `onlyOwner/onlyRole` on the function, or `require(target == <known constant/trusted module>)`.

lessons.lesson: Flag bypassable blacklist/denylist enforcement when the transfer gate includes an explicit recipient (or parameter) exception that lets a blocked sender complete the restricted action via the normal public transfer path (e.g., "blocked unless to == emergencyAccount"). Role-gating admin functions does not mitigate a logic-level bypass embedded in the user transfer predicate.
lessons.applies_when: In the ERC20 transfer path (transfer/transferFrom/_transfer/_update or a modifier/helper it calls), there is a blacklist/denylist predicate of the form `require(!blacklisted[from] || to == EXCEPTION_ADDR, ...)` OR `if (blacklisted[from] && to != EXCEPTION_ADDR) revert;` (or equivalent boolean logic) where `EXCEPTION_ADDR` is a mutable state variable (e.g., `emergencyAccount`) or a constant address, and the transfer function remains callable by arbitrary users (no onlyOwner/onlyRole gate on transfer itself).

lessons.lesson: Flag sentinel-based “infinite allowance” branches as an alternate authorization path: if `transferFrom(from, to, amount)` can skip allowance/consent enforcement when `allowance[from][spender] == type(uint256).max` (or `uint(-1)`), treat it as a concrete access-control bypass path for third-party debits.
lessons.applies_when: In `transferFrom(from,to,amount)` (or its internal allowance-spend helper), there is a conditional of the form `if (allowance[from][msg.sender] != type(uint256).max) { require(allowance[from][msg.sender] >= amount); allowance[from][msg.sender] -= amount; }` (or equivalent logic where `== max` skips checks/decrement), and the transfer proceeds to debit `from` and credit `to` regardless of that branch.

lessons.lesson: Do not clear CWE-284 for token/ledger access control when the snippet shows spending/withdraw sinks (transferFrom/withdraw) but does not include the full set of externally callable paths that create or mutate the guarded resource (balances/allowances). Missing credit/mint/deposit/adjustment entrypoints is itself evidence that alternate unauthorized mutation paths may exist outside the reviewed subset.
lessons.applies_when: In the provided subset: (1) there is at least one public/external value-moving or user-ledger sink that relies on resource-scoped checks, e.g., `withdraw(...)` that transfers ETH/ERC20 or `transferFrom(from,to,amount)` that debits `from` based on allowance; AND (2) the guarded resource state exists (`mapping(address=>uint) balances`, `balanceOf`, `allowance`), but the subset does NOT contain any complete, reachable set of functions that can credit/debit that resource (e.g., no `mint`, `deposit`, `increaseBalance`, `airdrop`, `setBalance`, `permit`, `approve`/allowance mutators beyond the standard surface), OR the code/comments explicitly indicate that funding/initialization/mutation logic is outside the shown code; AND (3) the analysis would otherwise conclude "not vulnerable" solely because the shown sinks use `msg.sender`/allowance checks.

lessons.lesson: Treat owner-renounce (or any owner setter) that writes the sole authorization root to address(0) as an access-control failure when other security-sensitive operations are gated exclusively by that root; unsatisfiable authorization is still CWE-284 even without an attacker bypass.
lessons.applies_when: All are visible: (1) an Ownable-style guard exists (e.g., `onlyOwner`, `_checkOwner()`, or `require(msg.sender==owner/_owner)`); (2) a public/external function callable by the current owner assigns `owner/_owner/admin` to `address(0)` (direct assignment or via `transferOwnership(address(0))`/`renounceOwnership()` with no `require(newOwner != address(0))`); (3) at least one other public/external security-sensitive operation relies solely on that guard (e.g., role/minter administration, withdrawals/rescue, pause/trading/router/pair/fee/exemption setters); and (4) no visible alternative admin/role or recovery/accept-ownership path can restore authority without passing the now-unsatisfiable owner check.

lessons.lesson: Do not treat `onlyRole(...)`/`onlyOwner`-style modifiers as proving access control when the modifier implementation or the role/owner administration surface is not present in the reviewed source; if sensitive effects depend on unseen inherited access control, classification must remain CWE-284-at-risk (not cleared).
lessons.applies_when: A public/external state-changing function reaches a security-sensitive sink (e.g., `_mint/_mintBatch`, balance/totalSupply mutation, withdraw/rescue, privileged config writes) AND the only apparent authorization is via a modifier/helper (e.g., `onlyRole(MINTER_ROLE)`, `onlyOwner`, `_checkRole`, `_checkOwner`) whose code is not included in the provided snippet (defined only in unseen parents/imports) OR the role constants/admin relationships are defined only in unseen code, so the reviewer cannot statically confirm (a) who can grant the role/ownership and (b) that the modifier enforces `msg.sender` correctly.

lessons.lesson: Do not clear CWE-284 when the only authorization on a security-sensitive setter is an inherited/undefined role/ownership modifier whose enforcement and role-granting/initialization logic are not present; treat the access boundary as unverified and flag as at-risk.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function writes security-sensitive configuration (e.g., `feeReceiver`, `treasury`, `taxWallet`, `router`, `pair`, `swapEnabled`, `limitsInEffect`, exemption/whitelist flags) and is gated only by `onlyRole(...)`/`hasRole(...)`/`onlyOwner`/custom modifier whose implementation is not included in the provided source (defined only in unseen parent/import), AND the role/owner assignment/granting path (constructor/initializer, `_grantRole`, `grantRole`, `transferOwnership`, admin-role wiring) is also not visible in the provided source.

lessons.lesson: Flag owner-gated external-address setters that also grant the chosen address spending authority over contract-held tokens (approve/allowance), because this converts an owner-only action into a permissionless drain path via the approved spender contract.
lessons.applies_when: All are visible in source: (1) an onlyOwner/onlyAdmin function sets a state variable holding an external contract address (e.g., `router`, `spender`, `module`) from a parameter; AND (2) in the same function or same code path, the contract calls `approve(...)` or directly writes `_allowances[address(this)][thatAddress]` (or uses `SafeERC20.safeApprove`) for a very large amount / `type(uint256).max` / `uint(-1)`; AND (3) the approved spender can initiate token movement from `address(this)` via standard ERC20 `transferFrom` semantics (i.e., approval is from `address(this)` not from individual users).

lessons.lesson: Treat user-reachable core execution paths (ERC20 transfer/transferFrom/_transfer/_update hooks) that WRITE to restriction/ACL mappings (blacklist/whitelist/exemptions/cooldowns) as access-control sinks: if the write is not gated by owner/role and is driven only by transfer context (pair involvement, isContract checks, thresholds), then any user can induce privileged restriction changes and CWE-284 should be flagged even if all admin setters are onlyOwner.
lessons.applies_when: In a token core path reachable from `transfer`/`transferFrom` (directly or via `_transfer`, `_update`, `_beforeTokenTransfer`): the code assigns to or mutates a restriction mapping/flag (e.g., `blacklist[x]=true`, `isExcluded[x]=...`, `cooldown[x]=...`) where `x` is derived from `from`/`to`/`msg.sender`/a parameter, and the surrounding condition uses only runtime heuristics (e.g., `if (preventBot && (from==pair||to==pair) && isContract(from/to))`) with no `onlyOwner/onlyRole` modifier and no `require(msg.sender==owner)`-style check on that path.

lessons.lesson: Do not clear CWE-284 on the basis of “all shown privileged functions are onlyOwner/onlyRole” when the contract contains explicit truncation/omission markers and the visible portion already includes at least one security-sensitive sink; missing code can contain alternate public/external entry points that reach the same sink without authorization.
lessons.applies_when: The provided Solidity source contains explicit incompleteness markers (e.g., "...", "// ...", "omitted", "rest of contract") AND within the visible portion there exists at least one security-sensitive sink (any of: ETH/ERC20 transfer from address(this), withdraw/rescue, privileged config setter for router/pair/treasury/fees, role/owner/admin update, whitelist/blacklist/exemption writes, pause/trading/limits toggles).

lessons.lesson: Do not equate “privileged” with onlyOwner/admin setters: flag any public/external function that mutates core token-global accounting invariants (reflection totals / supply-wide indices) without an owner/role gate, even if it only blocks a small class like “excluded” addresses.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function (not onlyOwner/onlyRole) writes to one or more global accounting variables that govern token-wide distribution/supply math (e.g., `_rTotal`, `_tTotal`, `_totalReflections`, `accRewardPerShare`, `rewardIndex`, `cumulativeFees`, global `rate`/`index`) via `=`, `+=`, `-=` and the only visible gate (if any) is a per-address exclusion/whitelist predicate such as `require(!isExcluded[msg.sender])` or `require(!excluded[msg.sender])`, rather than authorization by a privileged role or a resource-scoped entitlement check.

lessons.lesson: Do not treat “allowance is decremented” as proof of correct spender authorization in `transferFrom`; require an explicit sufficiency predicate (or a max-allowance sentinel branch) tied to `allowance[from][spender]` before the debit, otherwise the access-control boundary is implicit and can be triggered as a disruptive authorization failure.
lessons.applies_when: In an ERC20-like `transferFrom(from,to,amount)` (or its internal helper) the only allowance enforcement is a subtraction-based update such as `_approve(from, spender, allowance[from][spender] - amount)` / `_decreaseAllowance(from, spender, amount)` / `allowance[from][spender] -= amount`, AND there is no explicit `require(allowance[from][spender] >= amount)` (or `if (allowance != max) require(...)`) on that path.

lessons.lesson: Treat unconstrained constructor/initializer ownership assignment from an address parameter as an access-control failure when `onlyOwner` gates sensitive operations: the authorization root is externally selectable and therefore not provably restricted to an intended principal.
lessons.applies_when: In a constructor/initializer, ownership/admin is set from a function parameter (e.g., `_transferOwnership(_owner)` / `owner = _owner` / `_grantRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, _owner)`), AND there is no in-function constraint such as `require(_owner != address(0))` and no binding of that parameter to an expected deployer/authorized principal (e.g., `require(_owner == msg.sender/_msgSender())` or immediate handoff `transferOwnership(_owner)` from a known root), AND at least one public/external function with security-sensitive effects is gated solely by `onlyOwner`/that owner variable.

lessons.lesson: Treat external value-moving entrypoints as CWE-284-at-risk when their sole authorization depends on an unseen modifier/auth helper (e.g., `authenticateFor(sender)`), even if the callsite appears gated; do not assume the missing gate is correct.
lessons.applies_when: A `public`/`external` function that changes protocol state and/or moves assets (e.g., pool join/exit, withdraw/claim, mint/burn, transfers from `address(this)`) is protected only by a modifier or auth helper call (e.g., `authenticateFor(...)`, `onlyAuthenticated(...)`, `auth(...)`) whose implementation/body is not present in the provided source slice (defined in an omitted section, parent contract, or import that is not included), and there is no other inline `require`/`if` tying authorization to `msg.sender/_msgSender()` or a visible role/permission mapping within the function.

lessons.lesson: Do not clear CWE-284 when concluding safety depends on proving exclusivity of a single guarded setter for an authorization-root variable (owner/admin/controller/perp), unless you can statically enumerate all assignments to that variable within the provided source slice (including inherited/implicit write sites).
lessons.applies_when: The analysis rationale would be: “variable X is only changed by function F and F is gated (onlyOwner/onlyRole/onlyX)”; BUT in the provided source you cannot statically prove that F is the unique write site because any of the following are present: (1) inheritance is used (`contract C is Base`) and Base source is not included; (2) imported parents/libraries/modifiers are referenced but not shown; (3) there are ellipses/omitted sections/forward declarations; OR (4) the variable X is written via an internal function call whose body is not shown in the snippet. The variable X is the authorization root used by a modifier/check elsewhere (e.g., `onlyPerp` compares against `perp`).

lessons.lesson: Treat permissionless governance/execution triggers as access-control failures when they reach an arbitrary-call execution sink; time/threshold/hash integrity gates do not substitute for caller authorization.
lessons.applies_when: A `public`/`external` function has no caller-identity/role gate (no `onlyOwner/onlyRole`, no `require(msg.sender==...)`, no membership check) AND it deterministically reaches a generic execution sink such as `evmScriptExecutor.executeEVMScript(...)`, `executor.execute(...)`, `delegatecall(...)` to a script/target derived from stored motion/proposal data, or a low-level `.call(...)` using stored calldata/targets, even if execution is conditioned on non-identity predicates like `block.timestamp >= ...`, quorum/threshold flags, or `keccak256(script) == storedHash` checks.

lessons.lesson: Do not flag CWE-284 solely because a function changes an authorization-root variable (owner/admin/controller/perp) when (in the provided source) every write to that variable is guarded by a direct caller-equality check against the same variable and there are no other externally reachable write sites.
lessons.applies_when: All of the following are statically true in the provided source slice: (1) an authority root variable exists (e.g., `address perp/owner/admin`); (2) every assignment to that variable is either in the constructor/initializer or in functions protected by a modifier/require that enforces `require(msg.sender == <that same variable>)` (or `_msgSender()` equivalently); (3) there is no other public/external function (or fallback/delegatecall routing) that writes that variable, directly or via an internal helper; and (4) the sensitive operation in question is exactly that guarded assignment.

lessons.lesson: Treat “upgrade/deprecate + forward core ERC20 entrypoints to upgradedAddress” patterns as access-control surfaces: when `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`approve` remain permissionless but conditionally forward to an admin-set external contract (e.g., `UpgradedStandardToken(upgradedAddress).transferByLegacy(...)`), the authorization boundary moves out-of-contract and becomes unverifiable, so classify as CWE-284-at-risk even if all admin setters are `onlyOwner`.
lessons.applies_when: All are statically visible: (1) a state flag like `deprecated`/`upgraded`/`isDeprecated`; (2) an owner/admin-gated function (e.g., `deprecate(address _upgradedAddress)`) sets both the flag and a mutable `upgradedAddress` used for forwarding; (3) core public/external user entrypoints (`transfer`, `transferFrom`, `approve` or equivalents) contain a conditional branch `if (deprecated) { return UpgradedStandardToken(upgradedAddress).<method>ByLegacy(...) }` (or low-level call) instead of performing the local accounting; and (4) the code slice does NOT include the upgraded contract implementation and its `*ByLegacy` authorization (or otherwise does not constrain `upgradedAddress` to a verified trusted implementation).

lessons.lesson: Flag payout/withdraw functions that transfer contract-held assets to `msg.sender` when the only entitlement/authorization evidence is an external call (e.g., `tranche.transferFrom`, `vaultToken.withdraw`, `burnFrom`) and the contract performs no local, caller-scoped entitlement validation or accounting decrement before/with the payout.
lessons.applies_when: A `public`/`external` function (1) calls an external contract/token using a caller-provided amount (e.g., `IERC20(tranche).transferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), amount)`, `ITranche.withdraw(amount)`, `burnFrom(msg.sender, amount)`), AND then (2) executes a payout from contract-held assets to the caller (e.g., `stable.safeTransfer(msg.sender, x)` / `msg.sender.call{value:x}`), AND (3) there is no local per-caller entitlement state read+bound (e.g., `require(x <= claimable[msg.sender])`) and no local decrement/clear (e.g., `claimable[msg.sender] -= x`) tied to the payout in the same function/path.

lessons.lesson: Treat deprecation/upgrade forwarding in core user entrypoints as an alternate access-control boundary: once `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`approve` conditionally forward to an admin-set external `upgradedAddress`, local authorization invariants are no longer enforced by this contract, so the contract is CWE-284-at-risk unless the callee is statically constrained to a trusted implementation.
lessons.applies_when: All are statically visible: (1) a boolean/flag like `deprecated`/`upgraded`; (2) an owner/admin/role-gated setter sets `deprecated=true` and assigns a mutable `upgradedAddress` from a parameter; (3) at least one core public/external user entrypoint (`transfer`, `transferFrom`, `approve`) has a branch like `if (deprecated) { return UpgradedStandardToken(upgradedAddress).<method>ByLegacy(...); }` or a low-level `.call(...)` to `upgradedAddress`; and (4) there is no static trust constraint on `upgradedAddress` in the same contract (no immutable constant, whitelist/factory verification, codehash check, or selector filtering that ensures equivalent checks).

lessons.lesson: Do not treat “member-of-registry” checks (e.g., `strategies[msg.sender].active`) as sufficient access control when the function can transfer vault/treasury assets to the member; registry membership is not a resource-scoped authorization for shared funds.
lessons.applies_when: A `public`/`external` function is callable by any address that passes a registry/whitelist mapping predicate (e.g., `require(strategies[msg.sender].active)` / `require(isStrategy[msg.sender])` / `require(approved[msg.sender])`), AND the same function (or an unconditional internal call it makes) transfers ETH/ERC20 from `address(this)` to `msg.sender` (e.g., `asset.safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount)` / `call{value:amount}("")`), with no additional gate such as `onlyOwner/onlyKeeper/onlyVault` and no per-caller entitlement cap/decrement (`amount <= claimable[msg.sender]` with state reduction).

lessons.lesson: Treat deprecation/upgrade forwarding in core ERC20 entrypoints as a concrete access-control bypass of local restrictions: if `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`approve` can route to an admin-set external `upgradedAddress`, local pause/blacklist/limit checks no longer bound execution unless the forwarding branch itself enforces equivalent restrictions or constrains the callee to a trusted implementation.
lessons.applies_when: All are statically visible: (1) a boolean like `deprecated`/`upgraded`; (2) an admin/owner function sets that flag and assigns a mutable `upgradedAddress` from a parameter; (3) a core user entrypoint (`transfer`, `transferFrom`, or `approve`) contains a conditional branch that calls `UpgradedStandardToken(upgradedAddress).<method>ByLegacy(...)` (or low-level `.call`) instead of executing local accounting; and (4) the forwarding branch lacks explicit local enforcement of the same caller/resource restrictions that exist on the non-forwarding path (e.g., no `whenNotPaused`/`require(!blacklisted[from])`/`require(!blacklisted[msg.sender])` checks before forwarding) AND there is no in-contract trust constraint on `upgradedAddress` (immutable constant, whitelist/factory verification, or codehash/selector constraints).

lessons.lesson: Treat callback-style internal token/ETH payout routines as access-control boundaries when they pay `msg.sender` and are gated only by membership in a mutable allowlist; flag CWE-284 unless the caller is additionally bound to a canonical/derived identity (e.g., factory-derived pool address) in the same code slice.
lessons.applies_when: Source shows an `internal`/`private` function (often named `*_callback*`, `uniswapV3SwapCallback`, `pancakeCall`, etc.) that performs `IERC20(token).transfer(msg.sender, ...)` / `safeTransfer(msg.sender, ...)` / `call{value:...}(...)` (or pays a variable that is `msg.sender`), AND the only authorization is `require(allowlist[msg.sender])` / `require(_set.contains(msg.sender))` / `onlyPool` where the pool set/mapping is mutable via some admin/setter path, AND there is no additional check tying `msg.sender` to a canonical identity computed from immutable parameters (e.g., `require(msg.sender == IFactory(factory).getPool(token0, token1, fee))`, codehash check, or CREATE2 address derivation) visible in the same contract slice.

lessons.lesson: Flag shared-pool payouts that rely only on upstream external token actions for authorization: if a public/external function ultimately transfers assets held by this contract to msg.sender, but the contract does not perform any local, caller-scoped entitlement check/decrement and instead assumes correctness from an external token’s transferFrom/burn/withdraw, treat it as improper access control in this component.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function (1) executes an external call that consumes caller-provided tokens/claims (e.g., `IERC20(tranche).transferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), x)` / `burnFrom(msg.sender,x)` / `ERC1155.safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), id, amt, ...)` / `permit(...)`), AND then (2) triggers an external redeem/withdraw-like call (e.g., `tranche.withdraw(...)`, `redeem(...)`, `exit(...)`) that results in this contract holding/receiving an output asset, AND then (3) pays from this contract’s balance to the caller via `IERC20(out).transfer/safeTransfer(msg.sender, y)` or `call{value:y}`; AND within the same contract there is NO local per-caller entitlement state read+bound (e.g., `require(y <= claimable[msg.sender])`, position ownership checks) and NO local decrement/clear of that entitlement tied to the payout.

lessons.lesson: Flag permissionless triggering of role-restricted payouts: a public/external function that releases and zeroes an accrued role balance (fees/commissions) is a privileged action even when the payout recipient is a fixed stored role address; missing caller authorization is still CWE-284.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function (1) transfers ETH/ERC20 from address(this) to a stored role address variable (e.g., manager/treasury/feeReceiver), AND (2) the transferred amount is derived from role-specific accrued state variables (e.g., managerBalance0/managerBalance1/feesAccrued/commissionBalance) that are reset/cleared/decremented in the same function, AND (3) the function lacks any caller-identity/role gate (no onlyOwner/onlyRole/onlyManager and no inline require tying msg.sender/_msgSender() to an authorized principal).

lessons.lesson: Treat owner-controlled deprecation/upgrade forwarding of core user entrypoints as an access-control boundary shift: once `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`approve` can route to an admin-selected external contract, local authorization invariants no longer govern execution and CWE-284 should be flagged unless the callee is statically constrained or equivalent checks are enforced pre-forward.
lessons.applies_when: All are statically visible: (1) a boolean like `deprecated`/`upgraded`; (2) an owner/admin-gated setter (e.g., `deprecate(address upgradedAddress)`) sets the flag and assigns a mutable external address from a parameter; (3) at least one core public/external user entrypoint (`transfer`, `transferFrom`, `approve`) conditionally calls into that external address (e.g., `UpgradedStandardToken(upgradedAddress).transferByLegacy(...)` / low-level `.call`) instead of executing local logic; AND (4) the contract does not constrain `upgradedAddress` to a trusted implementation (immutable/whitelist/factory/codehash/selector filtering) and/or does not enforce equivalent local restrictions on the forwarding branch.

lessons.lesson: Treat owner-set upgrade/deprecation forwarding in core ERC20 entrypoints as an access-control boundary shift: once `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`approve` can route execution to a mutable `upgradedAddress`, local authorization semantics are no longer enforceable, so CWE-284 should be flagged unless the forwarding branch itself enforces equivalent restrictions or the callee is statically constrained as trusted.
lessons.applies_when: All of the following are statically visible: (1) a boolean flag like `deprecated`/`upgraded`; (2) an owner/admin-gated function sets that flag and assigns a mutable external `upgradedAddress` from a parameter; (3) at least one of `transfer`, `transferFrom`, or `approve` has a branch `if (deprecated) { return UpgradedStandardToken(upgradedAddress).<method>ByLegacy(...); }` (or equivalent low-level call to `upgradedAddress`) instead of executing local accounting; and (4) the contract does NOT constrain `upgradedAddress` to a trusted implementation (immutable constant, whitelist/factory verification, codehash check, or selector filtering), and does NOT re-apply local access restrictions (pause/blacklist/limits) on the forwarding branch.

lessons.lesson: Treat permissionless “factory/deployer” functions as CWE-284 when they also perform persistent registration/configuration writes into shared protocol managers/registries for the newly-created instance; the protected resource is the global manager/registry state, not the instance’s prior ownership.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function with no caller-identity/role gate (no onlyOwner/onlyRole and no inline require on msg.sender) (1) deploys/creates a new contract address (CREATE/CREATE2/new/clone), AND (2) in the same call path invokes external manager/registry contracts with setter/register-like methods that persistently store configuration for that new address (e.g., `registry.register*(newAddr, ...)`, `manager.set*(newAddr, ...)`, `guardManager.setGuards(newAddr, ...)`, `riskManager.setRiskProvider(newAddr, ...)`), where the manager/registry addresses are taken from contract state variables.

lessons.lesson: Model owner-triggered authority shifts through user-facing forwarding: an onlyOwner deprecate/upgrade setter that installs an arbitrary `upgradedAddress` plus a `deprecated` flag creates an indirect, permissionless path where any user call to `transfer/transferFrom/approve` can execute external logic chosen by the owner; this is CWE-284-relevant even though the setter itself is properly gated.
lessons.applies_when: All are statically visible in the reviewed source slice: (1) a state flag `deprecated`/`upgraded` toggles an alternate execution mode; (2) an onlyOwner/admin function sets `deprecated=true` and assigns `upgradedAddress` (or equivalent) from a parameter; (3) at least one core user entrypoint (`transfer`, `transferFrom`, `approve` or equivalents) contains a branch that calls into `UpgradedStandardToken(upgradedAddress).<method>ByLegacy(...)` or performs a low-level call to `upgradedAddress`; and (4) the code slice does not constrain `upgradedAddress` to a trusted implementation (immutable constant, whitelist/factory verification, codehash check, selector filtering, or equivalent).

lessons.lesson: Flag owner-controlled “deprecate/upgrade forwarding” in core ERC20 entrypoints as an access-control boundary shift: once `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`approve` can route to a mutable `upgradedAddress`, this contract’s local authorization/restriction semantics (pause/blacklist/limits/allowance handling) are no longer the governing checks.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract: (1) there is a boolean/enum state like `deprecated`/`upgraded`; (2) an owner/admin-gated function sets that flag and assigns `upgradedAddress` (or similar) from a parameter; (3) at least one of `transfer`, `transferFrom`, or `approve` contains a conditional branch that calls an external method on `upgradedAddress` (e.g., `UpgradedStandardToken(upgradedAddress).transferByLegacy(...)` / `transferFromByLegacy` / `approveByLegacy` or low-level `.call`); AND (4) the contract does not statically bind/validate `upgradedAddress` as trusted (no immutable constant, no whitelist/factory verification, no codehash check, no selector filtering, and the upgraded implementation code is not included in the slice).

lessons.lesson: Treat permissionless mutation of canonical global commitment/state roots (e.g., Merkle-tree branches/roots and their monotonic counters) as a security-sensitive access-control sink: if any caller can advance the counter and overwrite the stored branch/root, that is CWE-284 even if the contract has no withdrawals/admin setters.
lessons.applies_when: A `public`/`external` function is callable without `onlyOwner`/`onlyRole`/whitelist checks AND it (a) increments a contract-wide monotonic counter (e.g., `depositCount++`, `nonce++`, `index++`) AND (b) writes to a canonical commitment structure used as authoritative history (e.g., `branch[i]=...`, `merkleRoot=...`, `stateRoot=...`, `commitments[count]=...`), with no `require(msg.sender == ...)` or role/mapping membership gate.

lessons.lesson: Treat permissionless mutation of a contract-wide authoritative commitment root (or its direct inputs) as a sensitive resource requiring access control: if any caller can advance a monotonic counter and overwrite Merkle/commitment branches that determine the stored/returned root, flag CWE-284 even without withdrawals/admin knobs.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function with no caller-identity/role gate (no onlyOwner/onlyRole/whitelist and no inline `require(msg.sender==...)`) performs BOTH: (1) increments or otherwise advances a contract-wide monotonic index/counter (e.g., `depositCount++`, `count += 1`, `nextIndex++`), AND (2) writes to state that is used to derive an authoritative commitment/root returned by a view function (e.g., updates `branch[level]`, `roots[count]`, `merkleRoot`, `stateRoot`, or a `commitments[count]` array/mapping), where later consumers treat that root as canonical history.

lessons.lesson: Treat permissionless, payable state-advancing functions that update contract-wide commitment structures (Merkle branches/roots) as access-control-relevant even when no admin/withdraw functions exist; the protected resource is the integrity/capacity of the canonical state root.
lessons.applies_when: A `public`/`external` `payable` function (1) increments a contract-wide monotonic counter/index (e.g., `depositCount++`, `nextIndex++`) AND (2) writes to state used to derive/return an authoritative commitment/root (e.g., updates `branch[level]`, `roots[index]`, `merkleRoot`) AND (3) has no caller-identity/role gate (`onlyOwner/onlyRole/require(msg.sender==...)` or whitelist mapping check).

lessons.lesson: Treat "self-call only" (`require(msg.sender == address(this))`) as an unproven access-control boundary when the contract also exposes a public/external orchestration entrypoint (e.g., `execute`) that can cause those self-calls and the proposal/lifecycle authorization predicates are not fully visible; do not clear CWE-284 in that pattern.
lessons.applies_when: All are statically visible in the provided slice: (1) a privileged sink that can perform arbitrary external calls (e.g., `target.call{value:...}(data)`, low-level `.call`, `delegatecall`, or an `exec/relay` routine that forwards target+value+calldata) is gated by a "self-call" check like `require(msg.sender == address(this))` / `onlyGovernance` with `_executor()==address(this)`; AND (2) there exists a public/external function (commonly `execute(...)`) that triggers the privileged sink via internal calls (e.g., `execute -> _execute -> relay`), meaning external users can invoke the orchestration; AND (3) the authorization/eligibility logic that is supposed to restrict `execute` (e.g., `state()`, `hashProposal()`, voting/quorum/queue/timelock checks) is missing/unseen/ellipsized or depends on unseen parents/modules, so the reviewer cannot statically verify who can cause the self-call path to fire.

lessons.lesson: Treat external calls into a privileged downstream authority (e.g., Gnosis Safe) as access-control sinks: if any public/external function can invoke Safe state-changing methods (approveHash/execTransactionFromModule/enableModule/setGuard/addOwnerWithThreshold, etc.) without an explicit caller-authorization gate or signature-threshold verification in that same path, flag CWE-284 even if the function is “only” a lifecycle/rejection helper.
lessons.applies_when: In a public/external function, the contract calls a known authority-bearing external contract (e.g., `safe.approveHash(...)`, `safe.execTransactionFromModule(...)`, `safe.enableModule(...)`, `safe.setGuard(...)`, `safe.addOwnerWithThreshold(...)`) AND the function lacks any `onlyOwner/onlyRole`/allowlist modifier or inline `require(msg.sender==...)`/membership check AND there is no in-function signature/threshold verification step (no `checkNSignatures`/ECDSA-recover loop) binding the action to authorized signers (the only guards are non-identity predicates like proposal state/time/flags).

lessons.lesson: Do not clear CWE-284 when the only access check is `msg.sender` equality against an address *returned by an external contract call* (e.g., `require(msg.sender == exchangeConfig.upkeep())`), unless the returned address is statically provable as immutable/trusted within the same source slice.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external state-changing function reaches a security-sensitive sink (config write, fund movement, role/whitelist/exemption change, irreversible accounting change) AND its sole authorization is a predicate like `require(msg.sender == <externalContract>.<getter>() || msg.sender == <otherAddr>)` where `<getter>()` is an external call (interface/contract reference) and the code slice does NOT also show that (a) `<externalContract>` is immutable/constant or otherwise cannot be changed, and (b) `<getter>()` returns a fixed address not subject to admin setters/upgradeable proxy indirection.

lessons.lesson: Do not dismiss a permissionless "liquidate(target)" / "closePosition(target)" / "seize(target)" entrypoint as non-CWE-284 just because it has objective solvency/health-factor predicates; if the function can mutate a victim-selected account’s debt/collateral state and route any seized value to the caller, it is a cross-account sensitive operation reachable by arbitrary principals and should be flagged as improper access control (missing relationship/role scoping).
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function takes an address parameter used as the subject of a position mutation (e.g., writes to `debt[target]`, `collateral[target]`, `shares[target]`, `positions[target]`, deletes/zeros the record, or reduces balances), AND the function transfers any ETH/ERC20 to `msg.sender` (or credits `msg.sender` in a withdrawable mapping) as a liquidation reward/seized-collateral payout, AND there is no additional authorization binding between `msg.sender` and `target` (no `require(target==msg.sender)`, no role gate like `onlyLiquidator`, no per-user delegate/approval mapping `isApproved[target][msg.sender]`).

lessons.lesson: Treat permissionless cross-address checkpoint/record writes as improper access control even when the stored value is computed from deterministic contract state: unauthorized mutation of another principal’s history/state is a CWE-284 trigger independent of whether an attacker can “forge” the value.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function takes an address parameter (e.g., `delegate`, `account`, `user`) and writes to storage keyed by that parameter—such as `mapping(address => T[]) m; m[param].push(...)`, `m[param][i] = ...`, `delete m[param]`, or `m[param] = ...`—and the function has no guard that binds the caller to that parameter (no `require(param == msg.sender/_msgSender())`, no per-account approval/delegate mapping check, and no `onlyOwner/onlyRole/onlyKeeper`-style gate).

lessons.lesson: Do not treat solvency/eligibility predicates (health factor, undercollateralized, canBeLiquidated) as access control when a public/external function can mutate a victim-selected account’s position/debt; cross-account mutation requires explicit caller authorization (role/keeper/liquidator allowlist) or explicit per-account delegation.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function takes an `address`/`wallet`/`account` parameter and (a) writes to storage keyed by that parameter that represents a position/debt/collateral state (e.g., `debt[account]=0`, `positions[account].* = ...`, `_decreaseUserShare(account, ...)`, `collateralShares[account] -= ...`, delete/zero record), and (b) the only gating is a non-identity predicate such as `require(canBeLiquidated(account))` / `require(healthFactor(account) < ...)` / `require(isUnderCollateralized(account))`, with NO `onlyRole/onlyKeeper/onlyLiquidator`, NO `require(account==msg.sender)`, and NO `isApproved[account][msg.sender]`-style delegation check.

lessons.lesson: Do not treat `onlyRole`/`onlyOwner` as sufficient when the function performs user-scoped stake/position/reward accounting for an arbitrary `address user` provided by the caller; require resource-scoped consent (`user == msg.sender` or per-user approval) or flag CWE-284.
lessons.applies_when: A `public`/`external` function is gated only by a coarse global role check (e.g., `onlyRole(LOCKER_ROLE)` / `onlyRole(OPERATOR_ROLE)` / `onlyOwner`) AND it takes an `address user/account` parameter AND it mutates state keyed by that parameter (e.g., `stakes[user]`, `balances[user]`, `positions[user]`, `rewardDebt[user]`, `claimable[user]`, `locked[user]`) and/or triggers reward/asset transfer attributable to that parameter, WITHOUT any additional binding such as `require(user == msg.sender/_msgSender())` or `require(isApproved[user][msg.sender])` / `require(allowance/delegation[user][msg.sender])`.

lessons.lesson: Flag proxy-factory “arbitrary initializer” creation flows as CWE-284 at the factory/proxy layer: if any caller can deploy a proxy and immediately execute caller-supplied initializer calldata on it (typically via `address(proxy).call(initializer)`), the factory has exposed a security-sensitive initialization/configuration capability without caller authorization—even if the implementation (singleton) code is not shown.
lessons.applies_when: All are statically visible in the provided slice: (1) a public/external factory function deploys a proxy (e.g., `new Proxy(singleton, ...)`, `createProxy...`), AND (2) the factory accepts `bytes initializer` (or `bytes data`) from the caller and performs a low-level call to the new proxy with that data (e.g., `(bool ok,) = address(proxy).call(initializer)` / `proxy.call(initializer)`), AND (3) the proxy has a permissionless fallback/receive that forwards arbitrary calldata to the singleton via `delegatecall` (no `onlyFactory`, no selector allowlist, no init-only gate shown).

lessons.lesson: Model the exploit path for public proxy factories as: attacker chooses initializer calldata → factory deploys proxy → factory calls proxy with attacker calldata → proxy fallback delegatecalls singleton in proxy storage context. Treat this as an unauthorized reachability proof for privileged init/config code paths, not as “delegated authorization.”
lessons.applies_when: The source shows a callable sequence where (a) user-controlled `initializer` bytes are forwarded to a freshly deployed proxy via `.call`, AND (b) the proxy’s fallback/receive performs `delegatecall` to an implementation address stored/configured at deployment, with no visible restriction on who may trigger the delegatecall during creation.

lessons.lesson: Treat global-admin-only gating (onlyOwner/onlyRole) as insufficient for user-scoped ledger mutations when the caller can choose an arbitrary `user`/`account` parameter: without an explicit per-user consent binding, the function exposes unauthorized cross-account state changes even if only the admin can call it.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external state-changing function is protected only by a global authority check (e.g., `onlyOwner`, `onlyRole(X)`, `require(msg.sender==admin)`), AND it accepts an `address user/account` parameter, AND it writes to storage keyed by that parameter (e.g., `balances[user]`, `stakes[user]`, `rewards[user]`, `rewardDebt[user]`, `claimable[user]`, `locked[user]`, `positions[user]`) or invokes `_mint(user, ...)` / transfers value on behalf of that user, AND there is no additional binding such as `require(user == msg.sender/_msgSender())` or a visible per-user delegation/approval mapping check like `require(isApproved[user][msg.sender])` / allowance/permit consumption.

lessons.lesson: Treat caller-controlled payout redirection of escrowed/contract-held tokens as an access-control sink: a permissionless settlement/fill function must not allow arbitrary `destination/receiver` selection when the debit source is `address(this)`.
lessons.applies_when: A `public`/`external` function callable by arbitrary users (no `onlyOwner/onlyRole`/maker-only check) executes an ERC20 transfer where the sender is the contract (e.g., `IERC20(token).transfer(destination, amount)` / `safeTransfer(destination, amount)` / internal `_transfer(address(this), destination, amount)`), AND `destination/receiver/tokenDestination` is derived from a function parameter (or otherwise caller-influenced) rather than a stored order/position field, AND there is no inline check that binds `destination` to an authorized principal (e.g., `require(destination == order.maker || destination == order.receiver)` or `require(msg.sender == order.owner)` with destination fixed).

lessons.lesson: Treat contract-wide canonical ledger/commitment state (Merkle roots/branches, global indices, commitment arrays) as a protected resource: if any public/external caller can advance the global index and overwrite the stored commitment inputs that determine a publicly-consumed root, this is CWE-284 even when the function is framed as “bookkeeping” and there are no admin/withdraw functions.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external function with no caller-authorization gate (no onlyOwner/onlyRole/allowlist/eligibility predicate tied to msg.sender) performs BOTH: (1) increments/advances a contract-wide monotonic counter/index (e.g., `depositCount++`, `deposit_count += 1`, `nextIndex++`), AND (2) writes to state used to derive/return a canonical root/commitment (e.g., `branch[level]=...`, `roots[i]=...`, `merkleRoot=...`, `commitments[count]=...`) that is exposed via a view function/event for other parties to consume as authoritative history.

lessons.lesson: Do not treat “self-only” authorization (require(msg.sender==address(this)) / onlyGovernance with _executor()==address(this)) as preventing unauthorized reachability when there is any public/external orchestration function that can cause the contract to self-call the privileged sink during execution.
lessons.applies_when: All are statically visible in the same source slice: (1) a privileged sink function S (e.g., relay/execute/perform) is gated by `require(msg.sender == address(this))` or an equivalent onlyGovernance/onlyExecutor check that reduces to `msg.sender==address(this)`; AND (2) there exists a public/external function E (commonly named `execute`, `executeBatch`, `run`, `finalize`, `castVoteAndExecute`, etc.) that calls an internal execution routine which (directly or via an internal call chain) invokes S; AND (3) E has no caller-identity/role gate (no onlyOwner/onlyRole/whitelist) and its eligibility checks are non-identity predicates (proposal state/quorum/timestamp/hash comparisons) rather than restricting who may call E.

lessons.lesson: Treat an unconditional proxy `fallback`/`receive` delegatecall as a direct unauthorized reachability proof: if the proxy forwards arbitrary calldata via `delegatecall` to an implementation address with no caller gate and no selector filtering, classify CWE-284-at-risk even when the implementation (singleton) code is not shown.
lessons.applies_when: In a proxy contract, `fallback()` and/or `receive()` contains a `delegatecall` to an implementation address stored/assigned in state/constructor (e.g., `address singleton/implementation`) AND the delegatecall is unconditional with respect to `msg.sender` (no `onlyAdmin`, no `require(msg.sender==...)`) AND there is no function-selector allow/deny list (no `if (msg.sig==...) revert/allow`) before delegatecalling.

lessons.lesson: Flag proxy-factory patterns where caller-supplied initializer calldata is executed on a newly deployed proxy as an access-control failure at the factory/proxy layer (not a deferred implementation concern), because it is an explicit permissionless entry into implementation-defined privileged initialization/configuration logic.
lessons.applies_when: A public/external factory function deploys a proxy pointing at a singleton/implementation AND accepts `bytes initializer` (or similar) from the caller AND performs `(bool ok,) = address(proxy).call(initializer)` (or equivalent) without restricting the caller (no `onlyOwner/onlyRole`) and without restricting the initializer selector/calldata (no allowlist of init functions).

lessons.lesson: Do not clear CWE-284 for callback-style value transfers guarded only by a mutable allowlist/registry (e.g., `_pools.contains(msg.sender)`): if the slice does not also show a binding to an authenticated initiator/call context (or complete reachability), treat the access boundary as unverified and keep CWE-284-at-risk.
lessons.applies_when: The provided source slice contains an internal/private callback-like function (name/pattern indicates callback such as `*Callback*`, `uniswapV3*Callback`, `pancakeCall`, etc.) that transfers ETH/ERC20 (e.g., `IERC20(token).transfer(msg.sender, amount)` / `safeTransfer` / low-level value send) and its only authorization is membership in a mutable mapping/set (e.g., `_pools.contains(msg.sender)`, `isPool[msg.sender]`, `authorizedCallbacks[msg.sender]`). AND the slice is abstract/inherited/partial (e.g., `abstract contract`, missing children/parents/imports) such that (a) no public/external entrypoint path to the callback is shown, and (b) no in-function binding is shown between the payout and an authenticated initiator/context (e.g., verifying `msg.sender` equals a factory-derived pool address, verifying the callback is tied to a stored in-flight swap hash/state, or restricting the caller to an immutable trusted contract).
