lessons.applies_when: Source contains: (1) a stored sole-authority variable (e.g., `owner`, `_owner`, `admin`) that is used in an authorization modifier/check (`onlyOwner`, `require(msg.sender == owner)`); (2) an external/public function that sets this variable to `address(0)` (or equivalent unusable sentinel) (e.g., `renounceOwnership(){ _owner = address(0); }`); and (3) no in-code recovery/escape hatch for regaining authority after nullification (e.g., no `transferOwnership` callable post-renounce, no secondary admin role, no AccessControl role that can set owner/admin, no two-step reclaim).

lessons.lesson: Owner-only swap kill-switch does not prevent CWE-664 when normal user transfers are conditionally forced through a revert-prone external swap path with no in-transaction failure containment; treat this as a lifetime/availability resource that can enter a stuck phase once the trigger predicate becomes true.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) `_transfer`/`transferFrom` conditionally calls an internal swap routine (e.g., `swapBack`, `swapAndLiquify`) based on evolving on-chain state such as `balanceOf(address(this)) >= threshold` and a boolean like `swapEnabled`; (2) the swap routine makes revert-prone external calls (DEX router swap/addLiquidity and/or ETH forwarding via `.call{value:...}`, `.transfer`, `.send`, `Address.sendValue`); (3) there is no `try/catch` or low-level-call `success` handling that allows `_transfer` to continue when the external call fails; and (4) the only statically visible bypass is a privileged toggle (e.g., `onlyOwner updateSwapEnabled(false)`), with no user-accessible bypass and no auto-disable-on-failure latch.

lessons.lesson: Treat an ERC20 allowance granted by the contract as a lifetime-controlled permission resource: flag CWE-664 when the contract gives an external spender an unlimited (max uint) allowance and provides no in-code revocation/reset or spender-rotation mechanism, because the permission can never be retired after creation.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) in a constructor/initializer (or any one-time setup function) a call that sets allowance to the maximum value, e.g., `IERC20(token).approve(spender, type(uint256).max)` / `approve(spender, ~uint256(0))` / `_approve(address(this), spender, type(uint256).max)`; (2) `spender` is not `address(this)` and is an address used for external interactions (e.g., router/vault/bridge) or passed in as a parameter; and (3) there is no function in the contract that can later reduce/reset that allowance (no `approve(spender, 0)`, no `decreaseAllowance`, no `_approve(..., newAmount)` for that spender) and no function to rotate/change the spender address that would effectively retire the old approval.

lessons.lesson: Do not treat an owner-only swap/kill-switch as a mitigating bypass for revert-prone external calls executed on the user transfer path; if the transfer completes only after those calls succeed, ordinary users can be permanently locked out once the swap trigger becomes true.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) `transfer`/`transferFrom`/`_transfer` conditionally calls an internal swap/liquidity routine based on on-chain state (e.g., `balanceOf(address(this)) >= threshold`, `canSwap`, `swapTokensAtAmount`); (2) that routine performs revert-prone external interactions (e.g., `router.swapExactTokensForETHSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens`, `router.addLiquidity*`, or ETH forwarding via `.transfer`/`.call{value:...}`); (3) there is no `try/catch` and no low-level-call `success` handling that allows `_transfer` to continue on failure; and (4) the only statically visible bypass is a privileged toggle (e.g., `onlyOwner toggleSwap/setSwapEnabled`) rather than a non-privileged/automatic failure-containment path (e.g., latch disabling swaps on failure).

lessons.lesson: Auto-swap on the ERC20 transfer path that forwards ETH via a reverting primitive (address.transfer / Address.sendValue / unchecked call) without any in-transaction failure containment is CWE-664 even if there is an onlyOwner swapEnabled kill-switch, because the transfer resource can enter a stuck phase for all users once the swap predicate becomes true and the recipient/dependency reverts.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) `_transfer`/`transferFrom` conditionally executes a swap routine based on evolving on-chain state (e.g., `contractTokenBalance >= threshold` and `swapEnabled && !inSwap`); (2) after the swap, a function forwards ETH to a fee/dev/marketing address using `someAddress.transfer(amount)` OR `Address.sendValue(payable(addr), amount)` OR a low-level `.call{value: amount}()` where failure is not handled to allow continuing; (3) there is no `try/catch` around the swap/forwarding and no `if (!success) { ... continue transfer ... }` fail-open path; and (4) the only statically visible bypass is a privileged toggle/setter (e.g., `onlyOwner setSwapEnabled(bool)`), not an automatic/user-accessible bypass.

lessons.lesson: A persistent phase/lock flag that is set before an external call must be reset on every reachable exit path; any try/catch (or early-return) path that can bypass the reset is a CWE-664 lifetime-control bug because the contract can get stuck in the wrong lifecycle phase permanently.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a storage boolean/enum used as a phase or reentrancy/lock flag (e.g., `swapping`, `inSwap`, `locked`, `entered`); (2) assignment setting it to the “active/locked” value (e.g., `swapping = true;`) in a core/external user flow (commonly `_transfer`, `swapBack`, `claim`, `withdraw`); (3) a `try { ... externalCall ... } catch { ... }` OR conditional early-exit (`return`, `revert`-avoiding branch) that is reachable after the flag is set; and (4) at least one such catch/early-exit path can leave the function without executing a corresponding reset to the “inactive/unlocked” value (e.g., no `swapping = false;` on that path and no `finally`-equivalent pattern such as resetting immediately before every return).

lessons.lesson: Treat ERC20 allowances that the contract grants to external spenders as lifetime-controlled resources: if an operational function (deposit/stake/invest) sets or increases allowance for an external vault/router and no later code path revokes/resets that allowance, flag CWE-664 because the spending-right resource persists beyond the intended phase.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an externally callable operational function (commonly named deposit/stake/invest/allocate) that calls `IERC20(token).approve(spender, amount)`, `SafeERC20.safeApprove(token, spender, amount)`, `safeIncreaseAllowance`, or `_approve(address(this), spender, amount)` where `spender` is not `address(this)`; (2) at least one other function or lifecycle event suggesting the operation has an end/exit phase (e.g., a `withdraw/unstake/exit/close` function exists OR the deposit function is repeatable and not constructor-only); and (3) across the provided source, there is no code path that sets that allowance back to 0 for the same (token, spender) pair (no `approve(spender,0)`, no `_approve(...,0)`, no `safeDecreaseAllowance` to zero, and no spender-rotation that retires the old approval).

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a function persists a new “resource exists” record (e.g., creates a lock/position struct or increments totalLocked) before performing an external call that is required to actually acquire/fund/initialize that resource, unless the external call’s success is statically enforced (revert-on-failure with no catch/ignored return).
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a function that writes a new resource record or activates it in storage (e.g., `locks[id]=...`, `agreements.push(...)`, `positions[tokenId]=...`, `totalLocked += amount`, `active[id]=true`) BEFORE an external interaction that is necessary to make the resource real (e.g., `token.transferFrom(...)`, `safeTransferFrom(...)`, `delegate/undelegate(...)`, `router.*`, low-level `.call(...)`); and (2) that external interaction is not statically guaranteed to revert the whole transaction on failure because either (a) it is a low-level call with `success` unchecked, or (b) it is wrapped in `try/catch` and failure does not revert, or (c) it is an interface call whose boolean return value is ignored (no `require(token.transferFrom(...))`).

lessons.lesson: Treat same-keyed “auxiliary” mappings/structs as part of the resource lifetime: if a retire/revoke function flips a validity flag (or removes set membership) for key K but leaves other storage keyed by K intact, and other callable paths can still read/use that leftover storage without re-checking the retirement indicator, flag CWE-664 as stale state outliving retirement.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a per-key retirement action like `valid[K]=false`, `listed[K]=false`, `set.remove(K)`, or equivalent; (2) at least one other mapping/struct field keyed by the same K (e.g., `meta[K]`, `context[K]`, `cabinet[K]`, `params[K]`) that is not deleted/reset in the retirement action (no `delete meta[K]`, no resetting fields); and (3) at least one other externally reachable function (or internal function on a public path) that reads/forwards/acts on that leftover storage for K without first requiring the retirement indicator to be in the active state (e.g., no `require(valid[K])` / `require(listed[K])` / `require(set.contains(K))` on that path).

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a governance/claim execution phase recomputes a user’s/vote’s effective weight from live external AMM state (reserves/totalSupply) and performs time-delta division using (block.timestamp - storedStartTime) without explicit nonzero guards, because the weight resource can become undefined (division-by-zero) or manipulable right before execution rather than being controlled consistently across the proposal lifetime.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an execute/finalize function that calls into a weight/amount conversion helper; (2) the helper reads live AMM pair state via calls like `PAIR.sync()`, `PAIR.getReserves()`, and/or `PAIR.totalSupply()` (or equivalent external pool state); (3) the helper computes with a time delta expression like `block.timestamp - proposal.time` / `block.timestamp - startTime`; and (4) the code has no `require(block.timestamp > proposal.time)` and no `require(totalLP > 0)` (or equivalent guards) before any division by those values.

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a per-resource “retire/consume” flag is set only after an external interaction, because the resource remains in the active phase during the call window and can be re-used via reentrancy before retirement is recorded.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a per-resource validity/consumption flag in storage (e.g., `collected`, `withdrawn`, `claimed`, `closed`) that is used as a gate via `require(!flag)`/`require(flag==false)` in a claim/withdraw/unstake path; (2) in the same logical operation, there is at least one external call (direct `X.y(...)`, low-level `.call`, or an internal function call that itself performs an external call) executed before the first write that flips the flag to the retired state (`flag = true` or `flag = false` depending on convention); and (3) no `nonReentrant` (or equivalent reentrancy guard) is present on the entry function(s) that can be re-entered to reuse the gated resource.

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a monotonic sequencing resource (nonce/index/counter) is advanced and persisted before the contract successfully consumes the corresponding unit of work, and the contract exposes a “skip/clear” path that deletes the pending work without executing the consume action—because the sequencing slot is permanently retired without valid use (skipped work / desynchronized lifetime).
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a storage monotonic counter like `inboundNonce`/`nextId` that is incremented or assigned (`++`, `+= 1`, `= nonce+1`) and stored; (2) after storing, an external/virtual “consume” action is attempted (e.g., calling a user hook like `lzReceive(...)` / executing message/claim logic) that can revert; (3) on failure (explicitly via try/catch, low-level call success check, or a conditional that stores a `storedPayload`/`pending` record), the contract records pending work keyed by the counter value; and (4) there exists an externally callable function that can `delete`/clear that pending record (e.g., `delete storedPayload[...]`, `clearPending(nonce)`) without executing the consume action and without rolling back/decrementing the counter.

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 for ownership-lifetime phase overlap when a contract enters a renounce/expiry phase (flag or timestamp) but a two-step ownership acceptance function can still finalize a transfer without checking that phase, allowing ownership to remain transferable during its intended invalidation window.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a renounce/expiry mechanism that changes owner validity via a flag or timestamp (e.g., `isRenounced = true`, `_renounceTime`, or `owner()` returning `address(0)` after a time); (2) a separate two-step transfer mechanism with a pending owner (e.g., `_pendingOwner`) and an `acceptOwnership()` (or similarly named) function gated primarily by `require(msg.sender == _pendingOwner)`; and (3) `acceptOwnership()` lacks any guard referencing the renounce/expiry state (no `require(!isRenounced)`, no `require(block.timestamp < _renounceTime)`, and no equivalent phase check) while `renounceOwnership()` does not statically guarantee `_pendingOwner` is always cleared before `acceptOwnership()` can run (e.g., pending owner can be set in another function and accepted later).

lessons.lesson: Treat renounceOwnership/admin-nullification as a CWE-664 lifetime-control bug even without explicit “stuck funds” evidence: if the same codebase contains ongoing-onlyOwner lifecycle management entrypoints (pool/epoch/emission/parameter management, mint/burn control) and no alternate authority/recovery path, the authority resource is improperly retired and the system can be stranded in an unmaintainable phase.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a public/external function that sets the sole authority variable (owner/admin) to an unusable sentinel (typically `address(0)`) (e.g., `renounceOwnership(){ _owner = address(0); }`), and (2) at least one other public/external function guarded only by `onlyOwner` (or `require(msg.sender==owner)`) that performs ongoing lifecycle management (e.g., `add(...)`, `set(...)`, `updateEmissionRate(...)`, `massUpdatePools(...)`, `mint(...)`, `setMinter(...)`, `setRewardsPerBlock(...)`), and (3) no statically visible alternative authorization or recovery mechanism (no secondary role, no `AccessControl` role that can call those functions, no `transferOwnership` path executable after renounce, no timelock/multisig executor role).

lessons.lesson: Do not treat a per-record retirement flag (e.g., `cancelled`, `revoked`, `terminated`) as effective invalidation unless every resource-use/consumption entrypoint explicitly enforces it (or the retire function hard-retires the record by deleting/zeroing the consumable fields). If claim/withdraw paths ignore the flag, the resource remains usable after “retirement” and should be flagged CWE-664.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a per-resource struct/mapping with a boolean retirement flag like `cancelled/revoked/terminated`; (2) a function that sets that flag (e.g., `cancelVesting(...) { vesting.cancelled = true; ... }`); and (3) at least one externally reachable function that consumes the resource (e.g., `claim/withdraw/redeem`) that reads the record and can transfer/credit value, but has no `require(!record.cancelled)` (or equivalent) and the retire function does not `delete record` nor set the remaining-claimable amount to 0 (e.g., `amount = claimed`).

lessons.lesson: Treat factory-resolved AMM pair addresses as lifetime-controlled resources: if code calls `factory.getPair(tokenA, tokenB)` (or equivalent resolver) and then immediately casts/dereferences the returned address as a pair/pool without an inline `!= address(0)` (or `code.length > 0`) guard on the same execution path, flag CWE-664 because it is a statically observable use-without-availability-validation of a resource whose existence can vary over time.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains, in the same function and same control-flow path: (1) `address pair = factory.getPair(a,b)` (or `IUniswapV2Pair(factory.getPair(a,b))`), and (2) a direct external call on that address such as `IUniswapV2Pair(pair).getReserves()`, `pair.swap(...)`, `pair.totalSupply()`, etc., and (3) there is no guarding `require(pair != address(0))` / `if (pair == address(0)) continue/return/revert` (or `require(pair.code.length > 0)`) dominating that dereference within the same loop/branch.

lessons.lesson: Treat lifecycle/validity state as ineffective unless there is at least one statically visible resource-use entrypoint that enforces it: defining/mutating an allow/block/active flag (or critical “destination” handle) without any in-contract require/modifier checks at the points where the protected action would occur is itself a CWE-664 signal, because the resource can be used in phases the state was meant to forbid.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) lifecycle state variables such as `mapping(address=>bool) blocked/allowed/active`, `status` enums, or a critical handle like `proceedsDestination`/`treasury` that are written in one or more functions (setters, admin actions, status transitions); and (2) in the provided contract source, there is no external/public function that performs the corresponding resource-consuming action (e.g., withdraw/payout/execute/request) with a dominating `require`/modifier that reads those lifecycle variables (e.g., no `onlyActive`, `onlyNonBlocked`, `require(dest!=0)` on any callable action path).

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when privileged authorization is checked against a time-switching accessor (e.g., mpc()/governance()) that automatically flips to a pending role after a delay, while the canonical role storage (e.g., vault/minter) is only updated by a separate apply/accept function; this creates an implicit lifecycle transition that can grant authority without executing the intended explicit handoff step.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) privileged functions gated by a modifier/check like `require(msg.sender == mpc())` (or `onlyMPC`) rather than `require(msg.sender == vault)`; (2) `mpc()` (or equivalent accessor) returns `pendingVault` (or pending role) when `block.timestamp >= delayVault` and otherwise returns `vault` (i.e., a time-conditional role switch); and (3) a separate function (e.g., `applyVault()`/`acceptVault()`) is the only code path that writes `vault = pendingVault` (canonical role update), meaning authorization can switch even if the canonical role state is never updated.

lessons.lesson: Treat token-custody receivers as lifetime-managed resources: if a contract can accept ERC1155 tokens (implements IERC1155Receiver/ERC1155TokenReceiver) but provides an authority-only withdrawal that can transfer arbitrary ERC1155 ids/amounts without any storage-backed deposit record or release-phase constraints, flag CWE-664 because custody and release are not controlled by enforced lifetime invariants.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) `onERC1155Received`/`onERC1155BatchReceived` implementations (directly or via inheriting `ERC1155Holder`/`ERC1155TokenReceiver`) that return the selector and do not revert/validate `(operator, from, id, value, data)`; AND (2) an `onlyOwner`/admin-gated function that calls `IERC1155(token).safeTransferFrom(address(this), to, id, amount, ...)` (or batch variant) where `token/id/amount/to` are parameters or otherwise not fixed constants; AND (3) no mapping/struct is written on receipt to track deposits (e.g., `deposits[id][from] += value`, `allowedId[id]=true`, `lockedUntil[id]=...`, `isHeld[id]=true`) and no corresponding require checks in the withdrawal path enforcing such state (e.g., allowlist, depositor binding, timelock, retired/locked flags).

lessons.lesson: Do not treat a high-level interface call as proof that a CREATE2-deployed resource exists: if a factory persists a CREATE2-derived address into canonical registries (getPair/allPairs/etc.) without an explicit code-existence guard, flag CWE-664 because a non-deployed or selfdestructed address can be registered as a permanently “live” resource handle.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a factory function that computes/deploys a resource address via `create2` (inline assembly `create2(...)` or `new X{salt: ...}()`), storing it in a local variable (e.g., `pair`); (2) an external call on that address via an interface, such as `IPair(pair).initialize(...)` or `IPair(pair).setup(...)`, used as the only apparent deployment/validity gate; (3) after that call, the function writes the address into long-lived registries (e.g., `getPair[a][b] = pair`, `allPairs.push(pair)`, `pairs[id] = pair`); and (4) there is no dominating explicit guard `require(pair != address(0))` AND no code-size check like `require(pair.code.length > 0)` / `extcodesize(pair) > 0` before the registry writes.

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a per-instance phase predicate (open/closed/expired) depends on a mutable global duration/threshold that can be changed after instances are created, without snapshotting the parameter into the instance or restricting updates to times when no instances are active.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a global lifecycle parameter `P` (e.g., `voteTime`, `duration`, `expiryPeriod`) stored in contract state; (2) an externally callable setter for `P` (admin/owner-gated is still included) that can assign arbitrary new values and has no guard like `require(noActiveInstances)` / `require(totalActive==0)` / `require(block.timestamp > lastInstanceEnd)`; (3) an instance struct/record with a start timestamp `S` (e.g., `vote.startDate`); and (4) the instance validity predicate uses `P` directly (e.g., `isOpen = now < S + P` or `require(now <= S + P)`), and there is no per-instance snapshot field (e.g., `instance.duration = P`) used instead.

lessons.lesson: Do not convert “no executable logic present” (interface/abstract-only subset) into a NOT_VULNERABLE verdict for CWE-664; treat it as INCONCLUSIVE/NEEDS_IMPLEMENTATION because resource-lifetime control cannot be evaluated without state-transition bodies.
lessons.applies_when: The provided Solidity source contains only: (1) `interface` declarations and/or `abstract contract` declarations where all functions are unimplemented (end with `;`), and (2) no non-abstract functions with bodies `{...}` that write/read lifecycle state (flags/timestamps/struct validity) or perform resource use/retire actions; and the current rationale for not flagging is based on “cannot be evidenced from this snippet alone”.

lessons.lesson: Treat externally-resolved resource handles as lifetime-controlled resources even in getter-only contracts: if a contract exposes a “canonical” resource address by dynamically reading it from another contract each call (instead of pinning it in immutable/storage), and there is no in-contract stability check/snapshot, flag CWE-664 because the handle can change across time without this contract enforcing a consistent lifetime invariant.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a function (often `view`) that returns an address/handle by calling into another contract, e.g., `function COLLATERAL_TOKEN() external view returns (address) { return IRouter(ROUTER()).COLLATERAL_TOKEN(); }` or `return Other(resourceRegistry).getX();`; (2) the returned handle is semantically critical (token/vault/router/pair/oracle/controller) and is used or exposed as authoritative by this contract (returned publicly or used in internal logic); and (3) the contract does not store/snapshot that handle in immutable/storage at initialization time and does not enforce consistency via a guard like `require(IRouter(ROUTER()).COLLATERAL_TOKEN() == pinnedCollateral)` / `require(handle == expectedHandle)` on relevant call paths.

lessons.lesson: Non-atomic phase finalization is itself a CWE-664 trigger: if a function is responsible for ending a lifecycle phase (clearing a phase flag/timestamp) but performs any external call or token transfer before clearing that flag—and lacks an explicit reentrancy guard—treat it as vulnerable because reentrancy can observe/use the resource in a partially-finalized phase.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a storage phase indicator used as a gate elsewhere, typically a boolean/enum/timestamp like `phaseActive`, `finalAuctionEnd`, `locked`, or `inPhase` that is checked as `require(flag == expected)` or `require(flag != 0)` in other public/external functions; (2) a public/external “finalize/claim/settle” function that is intended to end that phase and includes an assignment clearing it (e.g., `flag = 0`, `flag = false`, `phase = CLOSED`); (3) at least one external interaction occurs before that clearing assignment on the same path (patterns: `IERC20(...).transfer/transferFrom`, `address(x).call{value:...}()`, or `OtherContract(...).foo(...)`); and (4) no `nonReentrant` modifier or equivalent reentrancy guard is present on that finalize function (and no obvious in-function lock set+cleared before the external call).

lessons.lesson: In upgradeable patterns, treat any operational function callable before initialize() as “pre-initialization phase”: if it reads a critical address/handle that is only assigned in initialize() (fee recipient/accumulator/router/controller) and uses it for transfers or external calls without an explicit initialized gate or nonzero/code-length check at point of use, flag CWE-664 for use-before-initialization of a resource handle.
lessons.applies_when: Source shows all of: (1) contract inherits Initializable/OwnableUpgradeable/UUPSUpgradeable or has an `initialize(...)` marked `initializer`; (2) `initialize()` assigns at least one critical address variable (e.g., `feeAccumulator = ...`, `router = ...`, `controller = ...`, `feeRecipient = ...`); (3) a core user-exposed path (`_transfer`/`transferFrom`/swap/claim/withdraw) reads that variable and uses it as a destination or call target (e.g., `super._transfer(..., feeAccumulator, ...)`, `IERC20(token).transfer(feeRecipient, ...)`, `IRouter(router).swap...`); and (4) that core path contains no dominating guard that initialization has occurred (no `onlyInitializing`/`initializer` restriction, no `require(initializedFlag)` set in initialize, and no `require(var != address(0))` or `require(var.code.length > 0)` immediately before the use).

lessons.lesson: Treat mutable fee-recipient/accumulator addresses used on every transfer as lifecycle-controlled resources: if `_transfer` always routes fees to a storage address and that address can be changed by an external admin function without being constrained by the same pause/freeze/phase guard that gates transfers, flag CWE-664 for phase-mismatched control over the fee-routing resource.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) `_transfer`/`transfer`/hooked transfer logic that unconditionally uses a state variable address as a fee destination or call target (e.g., `feeAccumulator`, `feeReceiver`, `treasury`) in a reachable branch (e.g., `super._transfer(from, feeAccumulator, feeAmount)` or `IFeeAcc(feeAccumulator).handleFees(...)`); (2) an external/public setter that can assign that variable (e.g., `setFeeAccumulator(address x)`), typically `onlyOwner`/admin-gated; (3) a lifecycle gate used on transfers such as `whenNotPaused`, `require(!paused)`, `require(tradingEnabled)`, or similar; and (4) the setter is not protected by that same gate (no `whenNotPaused`/`require(!paused)`/phase check), and there is no one-time initialization invariant on the setter (`require(feeAccumulator==address(0))` / immutable assignment / sealed flag).

lessons.lesson: Treat declared lifecycle flags as resources whose transitions must be visible and enforced: if the contract declares freeze/blacklist/eligibility-style flags but the provided code shows no reachable setters/clearers (or only setter without clearer), and core resource-use entrypoints do not consistently enforce the flag, flag CWE-664 because the resource’s phase transitions are not controllable or auditable in the visible lifetime surface.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a boolean/mapping lifecycle flag suggesting validity/phase (e.g., `mapping(address=>bool) frozen`, `notPaying`, `blocked`, `isActive`), AND (2) no function in the provided source writes both transitions for that flag (no evidence of both setting and clearing, or no writers at all), AND (3) at least one core user entrypoint that uses the underlying resource (e.g., `transfer/transferFrom/_transfer`, `claim`, `withdraw`, `mint/burn`) does not read the flag in a dominating `require`/modifier/hook check for all relevant paths (e.g., only checked in one hook but another entrypoint bypasses it, or only `frozen` checked but `notPaying` never checked).

lessons.lesson: Per-ID resource records must not be implicitly creatable/controllable via user-supplied identifiers: if a public function writes to a mapping/struct keyed by an ID parameter (fundId/positionId/orderHash) without first proving the record exists and that the caller is authorized for that record, flag CWE-664 as use-before-initialization/out-of-lifecycle control.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains: (1) a mapping or registry of structs keyed by an identifier type (e.g., `mapping(bytes32 => Fund) funds;`), and (2) a public/external function with an `id`-like parameter (e.g., `bytes32 fund`) that performs state writes to fields under that key (`funds[fund].balance += ...`, `funds[fund].cBalance = ...`, `funds[fund].status = ...`) or updates global aggregates derived from it, and (3) on the same execution path there is no dominating existence guard (e.g., `require(funds[fund].owner != address(0))`, `require(funds[fund].created == true)`, `require(funds[fund].lender != address(0))`) and no dominating authorization/binding guard tying `msg.sender` to that record (e.g., `require(msg.sender == funds[fund].owner)` or `require(fundOf[msg.sender] == fund)`).

lessons.lesson: Upgradeable contracts can have a statically detectable “divergent initialization path” lifetime bug: if a non-empty constructor initializes (or passes) a critical resource handle while an external initializer also initializes/binds the same handle, flag CWE-664 because the resource’s canonical binding can differ across lifecycle entry points even without pause/expiry flags.
lessons.applies_when: Source shows all of: (1) an upgradeable pattern (inherits `Initializable` and has an `initialize(...)` function marked `initializer`/`reinitializer`); (2) the implementation contract defines a non-empty `constructor(...)` (not just `constructor(){ _disableInitializers(); }`) that calls a parent initializer/constructor-like function with a critical handle parameter (e.g., `ERC4626Upgradeable(_asset)` / `SomeUpgradeable(handle)` / sets a storage handle); and (3) `initialize(...)` also calls an init routine or setter that binds the same handle (e.g., `__ERC4626Upgradeable_init(_asset, ...)`, `__X_init(handle, ...)`, or `handle.setSomething(address(this))`).

lessons.lesson: In EIP-2535 diamonds, treat the selector→facet routing table as the lifetime-controlled resource: if the DiamondCut constructor (or initializer) executes an arbitrary delegatecall-based init (initContract/initData) without statically visible code-existence validation on initContract, flag CWE-664 because the routing table and associated storage can be corrupted during the resource’s creation phase.
lessons.applies_when: Source shows (1) a diamondCut-style initialization in constructor/initialize that accepts or forwards `address initContract` and `bytes initData`, and (2) performs `delegatecall` to `initContract` (directly or via `initializeDiamondCut/initDiamondCut`) with `initData`, and (3) there is no dominating guard that `initContract` is a deployed contract (e.g., missing `require(initContract.code.length > 0)` / `extcodesize(initContract) > 0`) and no allowlist/pinning of initContract to a trusted constant/immutable.

lessons.lesson: Constructor-time external initialization combined with an always-available delegatecall fallback is a concrete partial-lifecycle window: if the constructor makes an external/delegatecall-based init (e.g., diamondCut/initializeDiamondCut with initContract+initData) before setting an explicit initialized/locked flag, and fallback dispatches based only on a nonzero facet lookup, flag CWE-664 because reentrancy during init can exercise selector→facet routing while mid-transition.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a `constructor` that performs an external interaction intended to initialize routing/state (e.g., calls `initializeDiamondCut(initContract, initData)` or executes `initContract.delegatecall(initData)` / `diamondCut(..., initContract, initData)`); (2) no storage flag/enum like `initialized/bootstrapped/locked` is set to its final value before that external interaction (i.e., the first write setting the flag occurs only after the call, or no such flag exists); (3) a `fallback()` (or other generic dispatcher) that does `delegatecall` to an address resolved from storage (facet/routing table) and gates only with `require(facet != address(0))` (or similar) and does NOT include `require(initialized)`/phase gating.

lessons.lesson: Caller-supplied indices used for per-user registry cleanup (array/set removal) are a standalone CWE-664 trigger when the function does not verify that the (value,index) pair matches current membership, because attackers can desynchronize “active resource” metadata from the real retired state.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an external/public function that performs terminal-state cleanup like `removeAddress(accountAssets[user], index)` / `arrayRemoveAt(userAssets[user], idx)` / swap+pop based on an index parameter; (2) the index (or both index and value) is provided directly/indirectly by the caller (function parameter), not computed solely from storage; and (3) there is no dominating validation tying the index to the intended element at time of removal (e.g., missing `require(userAssets[user][idx] == asset)` or missing `require(indexOf[user][asset] == idx)` / `require(contains(asset))` before mutation).

lessons.lesson: Treat “premature cleanup of authoritative active-set tracking” as sufficient CWE-664 evidence: if a function removes/clears an ID from the canonical ‘resource is active’ registry before performing the definitive retire/consume step (burn/close/delete) and there is any subsequent call that can revert or be skipped, the resource lifetime can desynchronize and must be flagged even if downstream implementations are unavailable.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a function that mutates an authoritative activity registry (e.g., `removeAddress(activeAssets[acct], id)`, `active[id]=false`, `delete activeSet[acct][id]`) indicating a resource is no longer active; (2) later in the same function (after the registry mutation) there is at least one external call or library call that is not statically proven non-reverting (e.g., a call to an interface/library function that could revert, token transfer, fee charge, `burn/close` routine), OR an explicit conditional/early-exit path that can skip the retire step; and (3) the definitive retirement/consumption write for that resource (e.g., `burn`, `delete position`, `debt[id]=0`, `consumed[id]=true`) occurs only after those later calls/branches, not before the registry mutation.

lessons.lesson: Multi-phase lifecycle enums must be enforced consistently on all sensitive operations: if one sensitive function requires the strict operational phase (e.g., `Active`) but another sensitive function only checks “not terminal” (e.g., `state != Extinct`), treat this as a concrete phase-mismatch lifetime-control flaw (resource usable while deactivated).
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an enum or discrete state variable with 3+ phases, including an operational phase (e.g., `Active`) and a terminal/retired phase (e.g., `Extinct`/`Closed`), plus at least one intermediate deactivated/paused phase (e.g., `Dormant`/`Paused`); (2) two or more externally reachable functions that operate on the same resource identity (e.g., `msg.sender`-keyed resource, `resourceId`, or `address peak`), where one function has a guard equivalent to `require(state == Active)`; and (3) another function that performs a sensitive stateful action on that same resource but only guards with `require(state != Extinct)` / `require(state != Closed)` / `require(!terminal)` (thus allowing the intermediate deactivated phase).

lessons.lesson: Treat conditional enforcement of a lifecycle gate as a phase-bypass vulnerability: if a transfer/claim/etc. phase flag is checked only inside a predicate that exempts specific privileged roles/addresses, then those exempted actors can use the resource before the enabled phase and should be flagged CWE-664 without needing extra “intent” context.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a phase/lifecycle flag (e.g., `tradingEnabled`, `isTradingEnabled`, `launched`, `claimOpen`) intended to gate a core resource-use operation (commonly `_transfer`/`transferFrom`/claim/withdraw); (2) the flag is enforced only conditionally, e.g., `if (isLimitedAddress(from,to)) { require(tradingEnabled); }` or `if (hasLimits(msg.sender)) require(tradingEnabled);`; and (3) the predicate function or inline condition explicitly returns false / skips enforcement for one or more privileged roles/addresses via static checks (e.g., `if (from == owner || to == owner) return false;`, `if (liquidityAdd[from] || liquidityAdd[to]) return false;`, `if (isPresale[user]) return false;`, `if (isExcludedFromFees[user]) return false;`).

lessons.lesson: Treat mandatory router/AMM external calls inside a fee/swap routine as a lifetime-controlled dependency: if an internal function that is (directly or indirectly) invoked from the token’s transfer/fee path performs router swaps/addLiquidity and has no in-transaction failure containment (try/catch or success-checked low-level calls that continue), flag CWE-664 because the token’s core operation can enter a permanently unusable phase when the external dependency reverts.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an internal function with fee/swap/liquidity semantics (e.g., `_takeFees`, `swapBack`, `swapAndLiquify`, `_swapTokensForToken1`, `_addLiquidity`) that calls a DEX/router/pair external method such as `swapExactTokensForTokensSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens`, `swapExactTokensForETH*`, `addLiquidity*`, `removeLiquidity*`, or any `.call{value:...}` ETH-forwarding step; (2) the function does not wrap those external calls in `try/catch` and does not use low-level calls with `(bool success, ) = ...call(...)` followed by logic that continues when `!success`; and (3) there is no statically visible alternative path in the same function that skips the external interaction on failure (no “fail-open” branch or failure latch/flag checked before calling the router).

lessons.lesson: Treat fee-recipient endpoints as lifetime-controlled resources: if a user-flow function unconditionally transfers fees to a configurable recipient, then allowing the recipient address to be set to address(0) (or other invalid sentinel) is a CWE-664 lifetime-transition bug because it moves the fee endpoint into an invalid/retired state while the fee-transfer phase remains active.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a core user-callable function (e.g., deposit/buy/transfer/_transfer) that computes a fee and calls ERC20 `transfer`/`transferFrom` (or native ETH send) to a state variable address (e.g., `feeCommissionAddress`, `feeBurnAddress`, `feeRecipient`, `treasury`), AND (2) an external/public setter that can assign that recipient variable from a parameter, AND (3) the setter lacks a dominating nonzero validity check (no `require(newAddr != address(0))`) before assignment.

lessons.lesson: Ownership-handoff (transfer/burn) must be treated as a retirement boundary for all value-bearing per-ID state: if a withdraw/finalize function transfers an ERC721/1155 position token away (or burns it) and then pays based on per-token stored accounting, but does not delete/zero that accounting in the same state transition, flag CWE-664 (stale economic state outlives the resource’s ownership lifetime).
lessons.applies_when: Source contains, in the same withdraw/finalize function and for the same `tokenId`/`id`: (1) an ownership handoff such as `safeTransferFrom(address(this), X, tokenId, ...)` or `_burn(tokenId)` (or ERC1155 burn), AND (2) a payout computed from storage keyed by that id (e.g., `metadata[tokenId].stakedETH`, `exitReward[tokenId]`, `amount[tokenId]`) that is read after or around the handoff, AND (3) there is no `delete`/zeroing assignment for all such value-bearing fields (and/or no `consumed[tokenId]=true` gate) on every successful path of that function.

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a phase flag temporarily disables the core binding/ownership check for caller-supplied resource identifiers (address/ID), because that “setup/open” phase becomes an explicit lifetime window where arbitrary resources can be used.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a storage phase flag (bool/uint/enum) such as `OPENING`, `initializing`, `setupMode`; (2) a resource-use function that accepts or decodes resource identifiers from calldata (e.g., `_vault`, `positionId`, `tokenId`) and performs an external action with them (e.g., `IVault(_vault).work(positionId, ...)`); and (3) the check that binds those identifiers to stored canonical IDs is explicitly conditional on the phase flag and skipped in one phase, pattern like `if (PHASE != OPEN && !isAllowed(_vault, id)) revert;` / `require(PHASE == OPEN || isAllowed(_vault,id));` where the OPEN phase permits any `_vault/id` pair.

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a monotonic lifecycle cursor (epoch/index/round pointer) is advanced even if the associated resource-use/release action is a no-op (e.g., claimed amount is zero), because callers can irreversibly skip a phase and permanently forfeit control over that resource window.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a persistent monotonic cursor/state variable such as `claimedEpochIndex`, `nextEpochToClaim`, `currentRound`, `cursor` that gates which IDs/epochs can be processed; (2) an external/public function that iterates or targets an ID/epoch and then advances the cursor unconditionally (e.g., `claimedEpochIndex++`, `cursor = cursor + 1`) on a success path; and (3) the function can reach that cursor-advance even when the resource action produced zero effect, evidenced by a computed `amount`/`balance`/`payout` value that can be `0` (e.g., `uint256 amount = ...balanceOf(...);` or `_claim(...) returns amount`) and there is no dominating guard `require(amount > 0)` (or equivalent) before advancing the cursor.

lessons.lesson: In timelock/governance contracts, treat any “pre-initialization” branch that lets an externally owned admin set `pendingAdmin` (or equivalent authority-handoff state) without going through the timelock’s queued/executed path as a lifecycle phase-bypass and flag CWE-664.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a boolean/flag used to distinguish an initialization phase (e.g., `admin_initialized`, `initialized`); (2) an admin handoff function (e.g., `setPendingAdmin(address)`) with a branch `if (!admin_initialized)` (or equivalent) that allows `msg.sender == admin`/`onlyAdmin` to set `pendingAdmin` (or equivalent authority transition variable) and also sets `admin_initialized = true` in the same call; and (3) the same function (or other admin-changing path) enforces timelock-style self-calls only in the post-init branch (e.g., `require(msg.sender == address(this))`) and/or expects admin changes to occur via queued execution, but the pre-init branch does not require `msg.sender == address(this)` and does not enforce any `eta`/delay/queue check.

lessons.lesson: Temporary “execution authority” (inExec) is not safe just because it is reset after the external call: if inExec-gated functions can permanently move position-owned assets to msg.sender (the current authority) without binding the recipient to the position owner, flag CWE-664 as an authority-scope/lifetime mismatch.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an execute-style function that sets an execution-scoped authority variable (e.g., `SPELL`, `EXECUTOR`) and/or a boolean session flag (e.g., `inExec = true`) and then performs an external call to that authority (e.g., `ISpell(SPELL).cast(...)`); (2) one or more privileged functions protected by `inExec`/session modifier that transfer assets (ERC20/721/1155) using `msg.sender` as the recipient (e.g., `token.transfer(msg.sender, amt)`, `safeTransferFrom(address(this), msg.sender, id, amt, ...)`) or that send borrowed/withdrawn funds to `msg.sender`; and (3) those privileged functions lack a dominating check binding `msg.sender` (or the transfer recipient) to the position owner for the active position id (e.g., missing `require(msg.sender == positions[POSITION_ID].owner)` or missing `to == positions[POSITION_ID].owner`).

lessons.lesson: Execution-context lifetime must end before any additional (potentially external) work: if a function sets global execution context (e.g., current position ID + authorized executor address) to gate privileged operations, then performs any further call after the main external execution returns but before clearing the context, treat it as CWE-664 because the context remains usable during that post-call window.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an entry function that writes global context variables such as `POSITION_ID = id;` and `SPELL = msg.sender;` (or similar) used by an `inExec`/session modifier; (2) at least one external call while context is set (e.g., `ISpell(SPELL).cast(...)` / `spell.call(data)`); (3) after that external call returns and before `POSITION_ID`/`SPELL` are cleared/reset, the entry function performs an additional call to another function (internal or external), e.g., `isLiquidatable(id)` / `check(...)` / `validate(...)`; and (4) at least one privileged function exists that is gated only by the context variables (pattern: `require(POSITION_ID != NO_ID); require(msg.sender == SPELL);`).

lessons.lesson: Post-spell callback reentry using still-whitelisted msg.sender is an explicit CWE-664 exploit path: if `inExec` authorizes by `msg.sender == SPELL` while global context remains set, any external interaction occurring before context clear can callback and invoke `inExec`-gated functions as the still-authorized SPELL, reusing execution-only resources outside the intended phase ordering.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an `inExec`-style modifier/check that gates privileged functions solely by stored context equality (e.g., `require(msg.sender == SPELL)` and `require(POSITION_ID != NO_ID)`); (2) an entry function that sets that context, makes a primary external call, and then makes at least one additional call before clearing context (as in the previous rule); and (3) the additional call is to a function not marked `pure` and not provably free of external calls (e.g., it reads storage and/or calls other contracts/interfaces), such that it is a plausible callback surface during the active context window.

lessons.lesson: Do not treat “factory-only initialize” as a mitigation if initialize can be called more than once: a privileged re-initializer that can overwrite persistent resource-handle addresses mid-life (token0/token1/router/vault) is itself a CWE-664 lifetime-reset bug when downstream fund-moving logic relies on those handles.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an `initialize(...)`/`init(...)` external/public function restricted by `require(msg.sender == factory)` / `onlyFactory` / similar; (2) the function assigns one or more persistent resource-handle state variables (e.g., `token0 = _token0; token1 = _token1;`, `router = _router;`, `vault = _vault;`); (3) there is no one-time/immutability guard in initialize (no `require(!initialized)`, no `require(token0 == address(0) && token1 == address(0))`, no `initializer` modifier, no sealing flag); and (4) there exists at least one other externally reachable function that uses those handles in value-moving/external-call logic (e.g., `_safeTransfer(token0, ...)`, `IERC20(token1).transfer(...)`, `IRouter(router).swap(...)`, `skim/sync/mint/burn/swap`).

lessons.lesson: Do not require an explicit external/public call path to flag a critical address-handle lifetime bug: if any reachable code path (including internal functions callable from public flows) can set a fee/treasury/recipient address to address(0) and core value-routing logic later uses it without a nonzero guard, that is already a concrete invalid-state transition followed by use.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a state variable address used as a value-routing destination (e.g., `_devWalletAddress`, `feeRecipient`, `treasury`); (2) at least one function (internal/external) assigns that variable from a parameter without `require(newAddr != address(0))`; and (3) a user-facing flow (e.g., `_transfer`, `deposit`, `claim`) uses the variable as a transfer recipient or external-call target without a dominating nonzero/code-length check (e.g., no `require(_devWalletAddress != address(0))` before `transfer(..., _devWalletAddress, ...)`).

lessons.lesson: Treat “orphaned retirement logic” as a lifetime-control flaw: if the contract defines internal-only resource destruction/retirement primitives (e.g., _burn/_burnFrom) but exposes no reachable external/public path to invoke them, flag CWE-664 because the resource’s end-of-life phase exists in code but is not controllable by any actor in the contract’s lifetime surface.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains an internal (or private) function that performs retirement/destruction of a core resource (e.g., `_burn(account, amount)` or `_burnFrom(account, amount)` that decreases balances/totalSupply or deletes a record), AND there is no external/public function in the provided contract that calls that internal retire function (directly or indirectly), AND the internal retire function is not an override required by an inherited external/public API in the same source snippet (i.e., no visible inherited public burn() wrapper).

lessons.lesson: Treat “timestamp reset as accrual boundary without settlement state” as a CWE-664 lifetime bug: if rewards/dividends accrue from (block.timestamp − lastTimestamp) and any function overwrites lastTimestamp for an active position without first persisting what was accrued (claimed accumulator / reward debt / paid-out counter), the accrual resource is effectively restarted without consumption tracking, enabling repeated or incorrect claims across lifecycle phases.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a reward/dividend computation that depends directly on a stored per-user/per-deposit timestamp as the sole accrual boundary, pattern like `accrued = rate * (block.timestamp - depoTime[id])` or equivalent; (2) a state-changing function (e.g., withdrawDivs/compound/harvest) that assigns `depoTime[id] = block.timestamp` (or similar) for one or multiple active positions; and (3) that function does NOT also update a complementary consumption/settlement variable keyed to the same position/user (e.g., no `claimed += accrued`, no `rewardDebt = accPerShare * amount`, no `paidOut[id] += accrued`, no `delete pending[id]`) before/at the timestamp reset.

lessons.lesson: Treat transient custody/release within a single transaction as a resource lifetime: unwrapping WETH to ETH (or otherwise converting a held asset) and then forwarding ETH to a caller-controlled recipient via an external call without reentrancy protection is a CWE-664 lifetime-control gap even if no persistent phase flags exist.
lessons.applies_when: In a single external/public function body (or its internal callees) there is: (1) a call to `IWETH(WETH).withdraw(amount)` (or equivalent unwrap/conversion of a temporarily held asset), followed by (2) an ETH send to a non-constant, user-influenced recipient address (e.g., parameter `to`) using `.call{value: ...}()`, `TransferHelper.safeTransferETH(to, ...)`, `Address.sendValue(payable(to), ...)`, `.transfer(...)`, or `.send(...)`, and (3) the function is not protected by `nonReentrant` (or an equivalent lock) and does not set/clear an in-function mutex before the send.

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 for reentrant “use-before-retire” when a withdraw/unstake/claim function performs an external token/ETH transfer before it marks the corresponding per-user resource record as consumed (delete/zero/claimed=true) and before it updates the aggregate accounting that enforces single-use.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains, in a single withdraw/unstake/claim function: (1) a per-user/per-index record read that gates execution (e.g., `require(deposits[user][i].amount > 0)` or `require(!claimed[id])`), (2) an external value-release call executed before record invalidation (e.g., `IERC20(token).transfer(user, amount)` / `transferFrom` / `call{value:amount}`), and (3) the record invalidation and/or accounting decrements that prevent reuse (e.g., `delete deposits[user][i]`, `claimed[id]=true`, `totalStaked -= amount`) occur only after the external call, with no `nonReentrant` modifier/lock on the entry function.

lessons.lesson: Treat per-user/per-ID retirement as ineffective if it occurs after an untrusted external token transfer: a withdraw/unstake/claim that sends ERC20/ETH out before it deletes/zeros the underlying position/record AND before it decrements the corresponding reserved/aggregate accounting creates a concrete lifetime window where the resource is still “live” during the external call.
lessons.applies_when: In a public/external withdraw-like function: (1) it reads a per-user/per-id record and gates on it being active (e.g., `require(deposits[user][id].amount > 0)` or `require(!claimed[id])`); (2) it makes an external value transfer call (`IERC20(token).transfer/transferFrom`, `safeTransfer`, or `call{value:...}`) to `msg.sender` or a caller-influenced address; (3) only after that call it performs the retirement writes (`delete deposits[user][id]`, `claimed[id]=true`, `amount=0`) and/or decrements global/accounting variables that represent reserved resources (e.g., `totalStaked -= amount`, `reservedInterest -= x`); and (4) there is no `nonReentrant`-style guard on the entry function.

lessons.lesson: Deterministically derived external resource addresses (e.g., AMM pair via `pairFor/create2` formula) must be validated against the authoritative registry (e.g., `factory.getPair`) before any state-changing interaction; otherwise the contract can operate on a non-existent/uninitialized resource handle (use-before-creation).
lessons.applies_when: In a function, the code (1) computes an address via a deterministic library/formula such as `pair = UniswapV2Library.pairFor(factory, tokenA, tokenB)` (or any `create2`-style computed address), and then (2) performs state-changing actions assuming that address is a live resource (e.g., `IERC20(pair).transferFrom(...)`, `IUniswapV2Pair(pair).burn(...)`, `mint(...)`, `swap(...)`, `sync(...)`, `skim(...)`), while (3) the same control-flow path has no dominating existence/initialization validation like `require(IUniswapV2Factory(factory).getPair(tokenA, tokenB) != address(0))`, `require(factory.getPair(...) == pair)`, or `require(pair.code.length > 0)`.

lessons.lesson: Treat the contract’s own token/ETH balance as an implicit lifetime-managed resource: flag CWE-664 when a function pays out by sweeping `token.balanceOf(address(this))` (or `address(this).balance`) after an operation, instead of transferring only the delta produced by that operation, because pre-existing balances become stale state that can be misattributed and repeatedly swept across calls.
lessons.applies_when: In a single external/public function (or its internal callees): (1) an operation intended to generate proceeds is executed (e.g., router swap, removeLiquidity, unwrap WETH), and then (2) the payout amount is computed as `IERC20(x).balanceOf(address(this))` or `address(this).balance` (or a local variable set equal to one of those) and forwarded to a recipient, AND (3) there is no pre-balance snapshot and delta calculation (`beforeBal = balanceOf(this)`; `afterBal = ...`; `delta = afterBal-beforeBal`) and no explicit reset/segregation mechanism ensuring the balance is zero before the operation.

lessons.lesson: Treat withdraw/unstake/claim as a lifetime-control vulnerability when it releases value via an external call before retiring (delete/zero/claimed-flag) the exact per-user/per-index resource record that authorizes the payout, unless the function is nonReentrant (or otherwise locks) on all reentry paths.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains, in the same public/external withdraw-like function: (1) a per-user/per-id record gate such as `require(deposits[msg.sender][i].amount > 0)` / `require(!claimed[id])`; (2) an external value-release call before retirement, e.g., `IERC20(token).transfer(msg.sender, amt)` / `safeTransfer` / `call{value:amt}()`; (3) the retirement write (`delete deposits[msg.sender][i]`, `deposits[msg.sender][i].amount = 0`, `claimed[id] = true`) and any aggregate accounting decrements occur only after the external call; and (4) there is no `nonReentrant` modifier and no explicit in-function mutex set before the external call and cleared after.

lessons.lesson: In signature/permit-style functions, treat nonce consumption as a lifetime transition that must occur only after all validity-window checks (deadline/expiry/notBefore) have passed; consuming the nonce first is a concrete lifecycle-ordering bug pattern to flag under CWE-664.
lessons.applies_when: A function (e.g., delegateBySig/permit/metaTx) (1) checks nonce equality using a post-increment or otherwise mutating expression, such as `require(nonce == nonces[signatory]++, ...)` or `nonces[user]++` before the final acceptance point, AND (2) performs any other validity-window or phase checks afterwards (e.g., `require(block.timestamp <= expiry/deadline)`, `require(block.number <= expiryBlock)`, `require(state == Active)`), rather than before the nonce is advanced.

lessons.lesson: Treat an unset privileged-role address as an intrinsic lifetime-phase bug when the contract exposes an authorization predicate for it: leaving a role handle at the default `address(0)` without an initialization invariant creates a persistent “pre-initialization” phase where downstream access control can be unintentionally disabled or mis-evaluated.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a storage variable intended as a privileged role address (e.g., `controller`, `operator`, `guardian`), (2) no constructor/initializer assignment to a nonzero value and no setter that is enforced to run before use (no `initializer`-gated init plus `require(initialized)` on readers), and (3) a public/external (or internal/library-facing) authorization predicate that directly compares against that address (e.g., `function isController(address a) public view returns (bool) { return a == controller; }` or `require(msg.sender == controller)` in a reusable modifier), without a guard like `require(controller != address(0))`.

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a signed-request “expiration” check for non-current nonces is anchored to a single per-signer timestamp/block that is refreshed by executing any current-nonce request, because that refresh unintentionally extends (revives) the lifetime of previously signed non-current-nonce requests.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a signed meta-tx/permit-like verification path that allows `req.nonce != currentNonce` (or otherwise accepts multiple outstanding nonces), AND (2) a staleness/expiry check for these alternative nonces that references a single per-signer anchor such as `_nonces[from].block`/`lastActionBlock[from]`/`lastSeen[from]` (e.g., `require(_nonces[from].block + tolerance >= block.number)`), AND (3) that per-signer anchor is updated on a different branch keyed to `req.nonce == currentNonce` (or any other unrelated success path), rather than being stored per-nonce/per-request.

lessons.lesson: Do not conclude NOT_VULNERABLE for CWE-664 solely because the visible contract is a thin wrapper that inherits lifecycle/phase-control modules (pause/burn/ownership) whose implementations are not included; instead treat the verdict as INCONCLUSIVE/NEEDS_PARENT_CODE (or at least not exonerated), because the entire lifetime-control logic boundary is outside the analyzed source subset.
lessons.applies_when: Source shows: (1) one or more `import` statements for lifecycle/phase-control parents (e.g., `ERC20Pausable`, `Pausable`, `ERC20Burnable`, `Ownable`, `AccessControl`), AND (2) the contract inherits them (`contract X is ...`), AND (3) the visible contract does not override the relevant lifecycle functions/modifiers (no local `pause/unpause`, `_beforeTokenTransfer`, `_burn`, `_mint`, ownership transfer/renounce overrides), AND (4) the parent contract source bodies are not present in the provided code under review (imports unresolved / not in snippet).

lessons.lesson: Treat “burn/retire by clearing owner only” as incomplete retirement: if a burn/retire path sets a resource’s `owner` (or equivalent authority field) to `address(0)` but leaves other liveness/value fields nonzero, then any later function that can mutate that resource without explicitly rejecting `owner == address(0)` is a concrete post-retirement control path and should be flagged CWE-664.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a retirement/burn routine that writes an invalidation marker like `record.owner = address(0)` (or `record.controller = address(0)`), but does not also clear a primary liveness/value field on the same record (e.g., missing `record.amount = 0`, `delete record`, or `record.active = false`); (2) a separate function that writes to the same record after retirement is possible (e.g., `record.owner = newOwner` / `record.amount = ...` / other stateful edits), and its preconditions only validate non-retirement properties (e.g., index bounds, tokenId exists) while lacking `require(record.owner != address(0))` (or equivalent retirement guard).

lessons.lesson: Treat owner-set reconfiguration in multisig-style contracts as a lifecycle boundary for outstanding approvals: if the contract changes the owner set or required threshold but leaves per-transaction approval mappings/counters intact for pending txs, approvals from removed owners can remain usable to execute transactions after their intended validity phase.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains: (1) a function that mutates the owner set and/or threshold (e.g., writes `_isOwner[addr] = true/false`, updates `owners[]`, and/or updates `_requiredApprovals` / `required`), AND (2) per-transaction approval state stored in mappings/counters (e.g., `_approvalStatus[txId][owner]`, `_approvalCount[txId]`, `confirmations[txId][owner]`, `numConfirmations[txId]`) that is used as an execution gate in an `execute`-like function, AND (3) the owner-set reconfiguration function does not clear, recompute, or invalidate that approval state for all non-executed/pending transactions (no loop over pending txIds, no deletion/reset, no “epoch”/versioning check binding approvals to an owner-set version).

lessons.lesson: Do not exempt onlyOwner/admin-only entrypoints from lifecycle/phase enforcement when they perform the same resource-consuming action as a gated public path; an ungated privileged “alternate path” is itself a concrete phase-boundary bypass under CWE-664.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains: (1) a phase/time gate state (e.g., `claimWait` and `lastClaimTime[user]`, or `unlockTime`, `isActive`) and at least one externally reachable function A that enforces it before calling a resource-consuming primitive P (e.g., `_withdrawDividendOfUser(user)`, `claim(user)`, `release(id)`), AND (2) another externally reachable function B that can also reach the same primitive P (direct call or via shared internal callee) but lacks an equivalent gate check, even if B is protected by `onlyOwner`/`onlyAdmin`/role checks.

lessons.lesson: Treat multisig owner-set/threshold reconfiguration as a lifecycle transition for *all pending approvals*: if the contract updates the owner membership or required-confirmations value but does not invalidate/recompute per-transaction approval mappings/counters that the execute path trusts, flag CWE-664 because approvals from removed owners (or under an old threshold) remain usable after the authorization resource has changed.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an owner-set/threshold update function that writes to an owners registry (e.g., `isOwner[addr]`, `owners[]`) and/or updates `required`/`_requiredApprovals`; (2) per-transaction approval state in storage (e.g., `approvalCount[txId]`, `numConfirmations[txId]`, `approved[txId][owner]`); (3) an `execute`/`confirmAndExecute`-style function whose gating condition is based on that stored approval state (e.g., `require(approvalCount[txId] >= required)`), without recomputing approvals against the *current* owner set at execution time; and (4) the owner-set/threshold update function does not clear/invalidate pending approval state (no deletion/reset loop, no version/epoch increment checked by execute, no “pendingTx invalidated” flag).

lessons.lesson: Treat ERC20 allowance updates as a resource-lifetime transition: if approve/_approve can overwrite a non-zero allowance with another non-zero value without enforcing a zero-reset (or using increase/decrease-only semantics), flag CWE-664 because the spend-right resource can be consumed both before and after the update via transaction ordering (classic approve race), meaning the old spend-right is not properly retired when the new one is created.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains an ERC20 allowance write path such as `approve(spender, amount)` calling `_approve(owner, spender, amount)` (or directly writing `_allowances[owner][spender] = amount`) AND there is no dominating guard that prevents non-zero→non-zero updates, e.g., missing `uint256 current = _allowances[owner][spender]; require(amount == 0 || current == 0, ...)` AND there is no alternative update-only API enforced (no restriction that updates must occur through `increaseAllowance`/`decreaseAllowance` while `approve` is absent or disabled).

lessons.lesson: Flag CWE-664 when a withdraw/unstake/exit function performs a resource-retirement/consumption side effect (burn/delete/close/external retire call) before it has established the caller’s entitlement for the requested amount via a dominating balance/position-sufficiency check.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains a public/external withdraw/unstake/exit-like function that: (1) takes a user-supplied `amount` parameter; (2) executes any retirement/consumption side effect before entitlement is proven, such as `token.burn(...)`, `_burn(...)`, `delete positions[id]`, `closePosition(id)`, or an external call whose name/semantics indicate retirement; and (3) the first caller-entitlement proof (e.g., `require(staked[msg.sender] >= amount)`, `require(position.amount >= amount)`) occurs only after that retirement side effect OR is only implicitly enforced later via `SafeMath.sub`/underflow revert rather than an explicit precondition.

lessons.lesson: Treat unresolved virtual token-lifecycle hooks as missing lifetime enforcement: if core token state transitions delegate phase/validity control to a `virtual` hook whose effective implementation is not available in the analyzed source unit, do not conclude “no lifecycle resource”; instead flag CWE-664 (or at minimum do not mark NOT_VULNERABLE) because transfers/mints/burns can occur without proven phase gating.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) `_transfer` and at least one of `_mint`/`_burn` call a hook like `_beforeTokenTransfer(from,to,amount)` (or `_afterTokenTransfer`) on every execution path; (2) that hook is declared `virtual` in the visible code; and (3) the provided source unit does not include any overriding implementation of that hook that enforces a lifecycle phase (no `override` body in the snippet, or the body is inherited from an import whose code is not present).

lessons.lesson: Treat state-stored external contract handles as lifetime-controlled resources at the point of use: calling a method on a storage-held address/interface without an explicit nonzero/code-existence guard in the consuming function is CWE-664 evidence even if higher-level phase/state checks exist elsewhere.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains: (1) a storage variable holding an external contract handle, typically `address` or an interface type (e.g., `UpgradeAgent public upgradeAgent;` or `address public agent;`), and (2) a user-reachable function executes an external call on it (e.g., `upgradeAgent.upgradeFrom(msg.sender, value)` / `IAgent(agent).doThing(...)`), and (3) on the same execution path the function lacks a dominating explicit validity check like `require(address(upgradeAgent) != address(0))` and also lacks a code-existence check such as `require(address(upgradeAgent).code.length > 0)` immediately before the call (i.e., it relies only on indirect checks like `getState()` / `canX()` that are not local to the callsite).

lessons.lesson: Flag upgradeable-token lifetime bugs when the upgrade gate is statically non-expiring: an upgrade pathway exists, but the function(s) intended to decide whether upgrades are allowed never consult mutable state (always-true), so the upgrade capability has no terminal/retirement phase.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a public/external upgrade entrypoint (e.g., `upgrade(uint256)`, `upgradeTo(...)`, `upgradeFrom(...)`) that triggers token migration or calls an `upgradeAgent`; (2) a gating predicate used by the upgrade state machine (e.g., `canUpgrade()`, `isUpgradingAllowed()`) whose body is statically constant-true (e.g., `return true;` and reads no state variables); and (3) no other storage flag/enum/timestamp exists that can ever flip upgrades permanently off (no `upgradesEnabled=false`, no `finalized=true`, no `upgradeWindowEnd`, and no branch in `getUpgradeState()` or `upgrade()` that can reach a terminal NotAllowed/Finished state).

lessons.lesson: Retroactive mutation of a time-lock boundary is a lifetime-control flaw: if unlock eligibility for per-instance locks is computed using a global mutable period at claim time (start + globalPeriod) and governance can change that period after locks are created, flag CWE-664 because existing resources can shift phases (locked↔unlocked) without a per-resource state transition.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a per-lock/per-deposit record storing a start timestamp but not a per-record end/unlock timestamp (e.g., `struct Lock { uint256 start; bool collected; ... }`); (2) an unlock/claim function that gates release with `require(block.timestamp >= lock.start + lockPeriod)` (or equivalent), where `lockPeriod` is a contract-level state variable; and (3) an external/admin/governance setter that can update `lockPeriod` after creation (e.g., `function updateLockPeriod(uint256 p) external onlyOwner/governance { lockPeriod = p; }`) with no restriction that it applies only to future locks (no snapshotting, no `require(noActiveLocks)`/epoching, no per-lock stored `unlockAt`).

lessons.lesson: Treat “retire-after-external-call” ordering in withdraw/unstake flows as a CWE-664 trigger: if a function that releases principal/value can be re-entered while the per-record retirement flag is still unset (because an external call happens first), the resource can be consumed multiple times in the same lifetime transition.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains, in the same external/public withdraw/unstake/exit function (or in a mandatory internal call it performs): (1) a per-record retirement/consumption flag check such as `require(!deposit.collected)` / `require(!claimed[id])` / `require(active[id])`; (2) an external call (any `X.y(...)`, low-level `.call`, or token transfer that can trigger a callback) executed before the first write that flips the retirement flag for that same record (e.g., `deposit.collected = true` / `claimed[id]=true` / `delete record`); and (3) no `nonReentrant` (or equivalent mutex) on the entry function.

lessons.lesson: Treat append-only commitments of caller-controlled identifiers as lifetime-controlled resources: if the contract records an externally supplied ID/commitment into persistent state (array/branch/root/log) using only a monotonically increasing counter and has no per-identifier consumed/uniqueness guard, flag CWE-664 because the same resource can be reused indefinitely with no retirement/consumption phase.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) an external/public function accepts a caller-controlled identifier/commitment (e.g., `bytes32 leaf`, `bytes32 deposit_data_root`, `bytes32 commitment`), and writes it into persistent state as part of an append-only structure (e.g., `leaves[count]=id`, `branch[i]=...`, `root=...`, `events.push(id)`) while incrementing a monotonic counter (e.g., `count++`), AND (2) there is no mapping/set-based uniqueness/consumption check on that identifier on the same path (no `require(!used[id])` and no `used[id]=true`, no nullifier set, no `indexOf[id]` existence check), regardless of the presence of a global capacity check like `require(count < MAX)`.

lessons.lesson: Treat storage-selected external “library/handler” addresses as lifecycle-controlled resources: if a function loads such an address from storage and then either (a) calls it or (b) treats it as authoritative for authorization (e.g., compares msg.sender to it) without a local nonzero/code-existence guard, flag CWE-664 because the handle can remain uninitialized (zero) or stale yet still be consumed as valid control state.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains, on the same reachable control-flow path: (1) a storage read that selects an external handle address or interface instance (e.g., `addr = defaultSendLibrary;`, `addr = uaConfig.sendLibrary;`, `lib = libraries[version];`, `IHandler handler = IHandler(addr)`), AND (2) the function either performs an external call via that handle (e.g., `handler.send(...)`, `addr.call(...)`) OR uses it in an authorization check (e.g., `require(msg.sender == addr)` / `if (msg.sender != addr) revert`), AND (3) there is no dominating explicit validation at point-of-use like `require(addr != address(0))` and (for call targets) `require(addr.code.length > 0)` immediately before the call/check.

lessons.lesson: Treat the governance voting-weight policy itself as a lifecycle-controlled resource: if an admin can toggle which weight sources (staking/vesting/lock/etc.) contribute to vote weight during an active proposal, and proposals do not snapshot that policy at creation, flag CWE-664 because the proposal’s weight regime can change mid-lifetime (creation→voting→execution) without an explicit phase transition.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a proposal lifecycle with timestamps/flags (e.g., startVote/endVote/executed/vetoed) and vote/execute entrypoints; (2) a vote-weight computation path (e.g., getVotingWeight/getSnapVotingWeight/_getVotes) that branches on one or more mutable state variables (e.g., booleans like stakingOn/vestingOn/timeLockOn or an address list of weight sources); (3) an external/admin-only setter (e.g., setVotingWeightSources/toggleStakingWeight) that can change those variables at any time and is not gated by proposal phase (no `require(block.timestamp < proposal.start)` / `require(!proposalActive)`); and (4) proposals do not store a per-proposal snapshot of the weight-policy variables used for counting (no fields like proposal.weightPolicyVersion/proposal.useStakingOn).

lessons.lesson: Treat constructor-time ETH fee forwarding as a resource-lifetime boundary: if deployment success depends on an external ETH send to a non-constant, non-this recipient, flag CWE-664 because the resource’s creation phase can be aborted by receiver-controlled revert behavior, preventing the contract from ever reaching an initialized/usable lifetime state.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains a `constructor` that is `payable` (or uses `msg.value`) and performs an ETH outflow to an address not equal to `address(this)` where the recipient is derived from a constructor parameter or other non-constant input (e.g., `serviceFeeReceiver_`), using `.transfer(...)`, `.send(...)`, or low-level `.call{value:...}("")`, and there is no alternative non-reverting path (e.g., no try/catch around `.call`, no "record debt/withdraw later" pattern, and no `require(receiver == trustedConstant)` constraint).

lessons.lesson: Treat an explicit per-resource retirement flag as mandatory for all consume/use paths: if a lifecycle transition sets `record.cancelled/revoked/terminated = true` (or similar) but any claim/withdraw/consume function (or its vesting/eligibility calculator) does not check that flag, flag CWE-664 because the resource remains usable after being marked retired.
lessons.applies_when: Source contains all of: (1) a per-resource struct/mapping includes a boolean retirement flag (e.g., `cancelled`, `revoked`, `terminated`); (2) at least one function writes that flag to the retired value (`flag = true` or `flag = false` depending on convention); and (3) at least one externally reachable consume/use function (e.g., `claim`, `withdraw`, `redeem`, `claimVestedTokens`) can transfer/credit value using that record but lacks a dominating `require(!flag)`/`require(flag == expected)` check, AND the helper that computes entitlement (e.g., `_calculateVestedAmount`) also does not reference the flag.

lessons.lesson: Treat pre-finalization accounting decrements as a lifetime transition that must not occur before untrusted/extensible hooks: if a function reduces an internal availability/backing counter (e.g., subtracts from a treasury/limit/quota) and then calls a plugin/hook that can revert or mutate the effective action amounts before the function finalizes the action, flag CWE-664 because the resource can be consumed out of order relative to the action’s lifetime.
