lessons.lesson: When multiple access control modifiers or functions contain overlapping validation logic for security-critical operations, where one modifier/function includes all the validation conditions of another plus additional role-based checks (e.g., onlyOperator vs onlyOperatorOrOwner), and there is no observable shared implementation or inheritance relationship that centralizes this logic in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more modifiers or functions in the same contract contain overlapping require statements or conditional checks for security-critical access control, where one contains all the validation conditions of another plus at least one additional role-based condition (e.g., operator check vs operator OR owner check), without observable shared function calls, inheritance, or centralized validation logic.

lessons.lesson: When multiple private functions in the same contract contain near-identical sequences of state variable assignments that set the same security-critical fee variables (e.g., _taxFee, _liquidityFee, _marketingFee) to different parameter values, and these functions are invoked through separate conditional paths (e.g., setBuyFee vs setSellFee) without observable shared assignment logic or synchronization mechanism that ensures consistent updates across all similar functions, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more private functions in the same contract contain identical sequences of assignment statements that assign the same set of state variables (identified by identical variable names across functions) to different parameter values, where those state variables control security-critical fee calculations, and there is no observable shared function call, constant definition, or centralized assignment logic that consolidates these sequences in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions or variables compute overlapping security-critical business values (e.g., tax rates, fee totals) through distinct but semantically equivalent calculation patterns, and these values are used in conditional branches or related operations without observable centralized validation or single authoritative calculation sequence in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions or state variables in the same contract compute security-critical business values (e.g., tax rates, fee totals) through distinct calculation patterns (different arithmetic expressions, conditional checks, or variable references) that produce semantically equivalent outcomes for overlapping operational contexts, and these values are referenced in conditional branches or related operations without observable shared function calls, constant definitions, or centralized calculation logic that establishes a single authoritative source.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions in the same contract or library delegate to other functions with identical delegation patterns and default error messages (hard-coded string literals) for similar low-level operations (call, staticcall, delegatecall), and these delegations create parallel code paths without observable centralized parameter definitions or shared constant values in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract or library contain delegation calls to other functions with identical hard-coded string literals as error message parameters for similar low-level operations (call, staticcall, delegatecall), and there is no observable shared constant definition or centralized parameter logic that consolidates these error messages.

lessons.lesson: When a security-critical validation function is called multiple times with identical arguments within a single transaction flow (e.g., within the same function or across functions called in sequence), and the return values influence overlapping security or business logic outcomes (access control decisions, trading validations, or protection checks), flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more calls to the same function with identical parameter values appear within a single function or across functions that could be invoked in the same transaction flow, and the return values are used in security-critical conditional checks, state modifications, or business logic calculations visible in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a function contains multiple conditional branches with near-identical sequences of security-critical validation operations (require statements, conditionals, or modifier checks) that differ only in which specific state variables they reference (e.g., _maxSellTxAmount vs _maxBuyTxAmount, _taxFeeOnBuy vs _taxFeeOnSell), and these variables control related security features (transaction limits, fee rates, or similar access controls) without observable shared validation logic or centralized calculation functions in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A function in the same contract contains two or more conditional branches (e.g., if/else blocks for isBuy vs isSell) that execute identical or structurally similar sequences of require statements, conditionals, or modifier checks for security-critical validation, differing only in which specific state variables are referenced within those validation sequences, and there is no observable shared function call, constant definition, or centralized validation logic that abstracts these checks.

lessons.lesson: When a public or external function contains only a return statement that calls another function with identical parameters and return type, and there is no observable additional validation, access control, parameter transformation, or logic beyond the delegation call visible in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A public or external function in the same contract contains only a return statement that calls another function with identical parameter values and return type, and there are no additional require statements, modifiers, arithmetic operations, state variable assignments, or other logic beyond the return call visible in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a public or external function contains only a delegation call to another function with a fixed parameter (e.g., msg.sender) and identical remaining parameters for a security-critical operation, and there is no observable additional validation, access control, parameter transformation, or logic beyond the delegation call visible in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A public or external function in the same contract contains a single call to another function where at least one parameter is a fixed value (e.g., msg.sender, address(0), a constant) and all other parameters are identical to the caller's parameters, with no additional require statements, modifiers, arithmetic operations, state variable assignments, or other logic beyond the delegation call.

lessons.lesson: When a function contains multiple conditional branches (e.g., if/else if blocks) that assign the same set of security-critical state variables (e.g., router addresses, fee exclusion mappings) with near-identical assignment sequences, differing only in the constant values assigned (e.g., different chain IDs, router addresses, or boolean flags), and there is no observable shared assignment logic or centralized configuration mechanism in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A function in the same contract contains two or more conditional branches that assign the same state variables (identified by identical variable names across branches) with near-identical assignment sequences, where the branches differ only in the constant values (chain IDs, addresses, or boolean literals) used in those assignments, without observable shared function calls, constant definitions, or centralized assignment logic.

lessons.lesson: When multiple public or external functions in the same contract contain identical sequences of security-critical delegation updates (e.g., calling _moveDelegates with identical parameter patterns after token operations like transfer, mint, or burn), and these functions share the same access controls, parameter validation, and core operational logic, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence in security-critical delegation tracking.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more public or external functions in the same contract contain identical sequences of function calls (e.g., _moveDelegates(from, to, amount)) after performing token operations (e.g., super._transfer, _mint, _burn), where the calling functions have identical modifiers, parameter validation patterns, and core operational logic visible in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions in the same contract invoke the same security-critical state update function (e.g., delegation tracking, access control updates, or accounting adjustments) with identical or overlapping parameter patterns, and at least one calling function contains additional security-relevant validation, conditional checks, or logic that is not present in the shared function or uniformly applied across all invocation paths, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract call the same internal or inherited function that performs security-critical state updates, and at least one calling function contains additional require statements, conditionals, arithmetic operations, or state variable assignments that are security-relevant and not present in the shared function or in other calling functions.

lessons.lesson: When a contract contains multiple state variables that store identical or semantically equivalent security-critical values (e.g., fee totals, tax accumulations, or similar accounting data) and are referenced in overlapping security-critical calculations without observable synchronization logic that guarantees their equality across all code paths, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more state variables in the same contract store identical or semantically equivalent values (identified by identical assignment statements like `varA = varB` or identical constant initializations), both variables are referenced in security-critical calculations (e.g., fee sums, balance adjustments, or access decisions) visible in the source code, and there is no observable shared function call, inheritance relationship, or centralized update logic that ensures their values remain synchronized when modified.

lessons.lesson: When multiple internal pure functions in the same contract contain identical or structurally similar sequences of parameter assignments to fields with identical or semantically equivalent names across different data structure types (e.g., DTOs, structs), and these functions are used to construct objects for business logic operations without observable shared implementation or centralized assignment logic, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more internal pure functions in the same contract contain identical or structurally similar sequences of parameter assignments (e.g., field = parameter) where the field names are identical or semantically equivalent across different data structure types, and there is no observable shared function call, constant definition, or centralized assignment logic that consolidates these sequences in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When identical arithmetic expressions that compute security-critical totals (e.g., fee sums, tax accumulations) appear in both state variable initialization (declaration or constructor) and a function that updates those totals, and there is no observable centralized calculation function, constant definition, or synchronization mechanism that ensures consistency between the initialization and update paths in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A state variable storing a security-critical total (e.g., totalFee, totalTax) is initialized with an arithmetic expression (e.g., marketingFee + otherFee) in its declaration or constructor, and a separate function (e.g., setFees) contains an identical arithmetic expression to compute and assign the same total, without observable shared calculation logic or synchronization between the initialization and update paths.

lessons.lesson: When a state variable stores a derived value computed from other state variables through an arithmetic expression, and there exists a separate function that recomputes and updates that derived value using an identical or semantically equivalent arithmetic expression, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A state variable is initialized with an arithmetic expression (e.g., totalFee = marketingFee + otherFee) in its declaration or constructor, and a separate function (e.g., setFees) contains an identical arithmetic expression to compute and assign the same derived value, without observable shared calculation logic or automatic synchronization that ensures the derived value updates whenever the base variables change elsewhere in the contract.

lessons.lesson: When a derived state variable storing a security-critical total (e.g., total fee, total tax) is initialized with an arithmetic expression in its declaration or constructor, and a separate function contains an identical or structurally similar arithmetic expression to compute and assign the same derived value, and there is no observable shared calculation function, constant definition, or automatic synchronization mechanism that ensures the derived value updates whenever the base variables change elsewhere in the contract, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A state variable is initialized with an arithmetic expression (e.g., totalFee = marketingFee + otherFee) in its declaration or constructor, and a separate function (e.g., setFees) contains an identical or structurally similar arithmetic expression to compute and assign the same derived value, without observable shared calculation logic or automatic synchronization that ensures the derived value updates whenever the base variables change elsewhere in the contract.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions in the same contract share similar structural patterns (identical modifiers, require statements, state assignments, and event emissions) for security-critical operations on different but related state variables or roles, and these functions lack observable shared implementation or centralized logic that consolidates these patterns, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract have identical modifiers (e.g., 'onlyOwner'), identical sequences of require statements with similar logical conditions, identical state assignment patterns (e.g., mapping[address] = parameter), and identical event emission patterns, but target different state variables or mappings that control related security-critical features (access control, transaction limits, fee exclusions), without observable shared function calls, inheritance relationships, or centralized logic in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a library or contract contains overloaded functions that delegate to a common function with default parameters (e.g., two-parameter arithmetic functions calling three-parameter versions with fixed error messages or values), and there is no observable contextual separation in call sites, parameter types, or access controls that would prevent independent modification of the delegating functions, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same library or contract contain delegation calls to a common function with identical or structurally similar parameter patterns, where the delegating functions differ only in providing default values (e.g., hard-coded error message strings, numeric literals, or boolean flags) for one or more parameters, and there is no observable contextual separation in function signatures, call patterns, or access controls in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a contract inherits from a parent contract that defines a state variable and provides functions to manage it (e.g., setter functions, modifiers, or validation logic), and the child contract's constructor or initialization function contains a direct assignment to that same state variable without calling the parent's management functions or including observable synchronization logic in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A contract inherits from a parent contract that defines a state variable and provides functions to manage it (e.g., setter functions, modifiers, or validation logic), and the child contract's constructor or initialization function contains a direct assignment to that same state variable without calling the parent's management functions or including observable synchronization logic in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a child contract defines a function with identical name and security-critical purpose to a function in its parent contract, and the child function contains identical or overlapping implementation logic without observable delegation to the parent function or a shared internal function that centralizes the logic, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A child contract contains a function with the same name and security-critical purpose as a function in its parent contract, and the child function's implementation includes identical or overlapping sequences of state variable assignments, arithmetic operations, or external calls without observable calls to the parent function or a shared internal function that consolidates this logic in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a child contract's constructor or initialization function directly assigns a value to a state variable that is also defined and managed in an inherited parent contract through dedicated functions, and there is no observable call to the parent's management functions or synchronization logic that ensures consistency between the two assignment paths, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A child contract's constructor or initialization function contains a direct assignment to a state variable that is also defined in a parent contract, where the parent contract provides functions to manage that variable (e.g., setter functions with validation), and the child's assignment does not call those parent functions or include observable synchronization logic in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When two or more functions in the same contract contain identical or structurally similar sequences of state variable assignments, arithmetic operations, or external calls that perform security-critical operations (balance updates, fee calculations, access checks), and these functions are invoked through separate conditional paths or distinct entry points without observable shared implementation, inheritance relationship, or centralized synchronization logic that ensures consistent updates across all invocation contexts, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with concrete risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract contain identical or structurally similar sequences of state variable assignments, arithmetic operations, or external calls that perform security-critical operations, are called from different conditional branches or separate entry points (e.g., _basicTransfer vs _transfer), and there is no observable shared function call, inheritance relationship, or centralized logic that consolidates these sequences in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple public or external functions in the same contract call the same internal function that performs security-critical operations (e.g., role management, access control, state transitions), and at least one calling function contains additional security-relevant validation, conditional checks, or logic that is not present in the shared function or uniformly applied across all invocation paths, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more public or external functions in the same contract call the same internal function that performs security-critical operations, and at least one calling function contains additional require statements, conditionals, arithmetic operations, or state variable assignments that are security-relevant and not present in the shared function or in other calling functions.

lessons.lesson: When a library or contract contains multiple functions that perform identical core arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) with identical parameter structures and overflow/underflow validation logic, but differ only in error-handling mechanisms (e.g., try-catch blocks returning boolean vs. require statements reverting on overflow) or return types (e.g., bool vs uint256), and there is no observable contextual separation (distinct parameter types, access controls, non-overlapping call sites, or clear documentation of distinct purposes) visible in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same library or contract contain identical sequences of arithmetic operations and overflow/underflow validation logic, differ only in error-handling implementations (e.g., try-catch blocks returning bool vs. require statements reverting on overflow) or return types (e.g., bool vs uint256), and there is no observable contextual separation in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a function contains conditional branches (e.g., for buy vs sell contexts) that compute security-critical values (tax rates, fee totals, or similar accounting amounts) using identical or structurally similar arithmetic expressions (e.g., amount.mul(...).div(100)), differing only in which specific state variables they reference (e.g., _taxFeeOnBuy vs _taxFeeOnSell, _reduceBuyTaxAt vs _reduceSellTaxAt), and there is no observable shared function call, constant definition, or centralized calculation logic that abstracts these expressions in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A function in the same contract contains two or more conditional branches that compute security-critical values using identical or structurally similar arithmetic expressions, differing only in which specific state variables or constants are referenced within those expressions, without observable shared calculation logic in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple interfaces in the same codebase contain identical event declarations (identical event names and parameter types) for security-critical operations (e.g., token transfers, approvals, or state transitions), and there is no observable shared inheritance relationship, abstract contract, or centralized event definition that synchronizes these declarations in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence in event handling.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more interfaces in the same codebase contain identical event declarations (identical event names and parameter types) for security-critical operations, and there is no observable inheritance relationship (one interface does not inherit from the other), abstract contract that both implement, or shared event definition that centralizes these declarations.

lessons.lesson: When multiple external functions in the same contract delegate to the same internal function for security-critical operations, and at least one calling function contains additional security-relevant validation, conditional checks, or logic that is not present in the shared function or uniformly applied across all invocation paths, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more external functions in the same contract call the same internal function that performs security-critical operations, and at least one calling function contains additional require statements, conditionals, arithmetic operations, or state variable assignments that are security-relevant and not present in the shared function or in other calling functions.

lessons.lesson: When a state variable is updated in both a modifier and the function it modifies, using different expressions that could evaluate to distinct values (e.g., one using a function call like lastTimeRewardApplicable() and another using block.timestamp), and these updates influence overlapping security-critical business logic (e.g., reward calculations, fee accumulations, or access decisions) without observable synchronization or single authoritative assignment path in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A modifier contains an assignment to a state variable using an expression (e.g., function call, arithmetic operation, or conditional result), and a function modified by that modifier contains another assignment to the same state variable using a different expression, where both assignments influence security-critical business logic visible in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When identical validation sequences (require statements, conditionals, or modifier checks) with identical logical conditions and error messages appear in multiple functions within the same contract, and these sequences validate security-critical conditions (e.g., zero-address checks, balance validations, or access control conditions), flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence, regardless of the functions' operational differences or contextual separation.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract contain identical require statements, conditionals, or modifier checks with identical logical conditions and error message strings that validate security-critical parameters, and these functions perform distinct but related security operations visible in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a contract contains multiple conditional branches (e.g., for buy vs sell contexts) that compute security-critical values (tax rates, fee totals, or similar accounting amounts) using identical or structurally similar arithmetic expressions (e.g., amount.mul(...).div(100)), differing only in which specific state variables they reference (e.g., _taxFeeOnBuy vs _taxFeeOnSell, _reduceBuyTaxAt vs _reduceSellTaxAt), and there is no observable shared function call, constant definition, or centralized calculation logic that abstracts these expressions in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A function in the same contract contains two or more conditional branches that compute security-critical values using identical or structurally similar arithmetic expressions, differing only in which specific state variables or constants are referenced within those expressions, without observable shared calculation logic in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple overloaded functions in the same library or contract delegate to a common function with identical core security-critical logic sequences (arithmetic operations, overflow/underflow validation, or similar validation patterns), but the delegating functions contain identical or structurally similar parameter preparation, error message handling, or return type adaptation logic that is not centralized in the common function or elsewhere in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more overloaded functions in the same library or contract call a common function with identical or semantically equivalent parameters for security-critical operations, and each delegating function contains identical sequences of parameter extraction, type conversion, error message assignment, or return value adaptation that is not present in the common function or consolidated into a shared helper function visible in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple function overloads in the same contract or library delegate to a common function with identical core security-critical logic sequences (arithmetic operations, overflow/underflow validation, or similar validation patterns), but the delegating functions contain identical or structurally similar hard-coded default values (such as error message strings, numeric literals, or boolean flags) that influence security-critical operations, and these default values are not defined as shared constants or centralized in a single location visible in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more function overloads in the same contract or library call a common function with identical or semantically equivalent parameters for security-critical operations, and each delegating function contains identical hard-coded string literals, numeric literals, or boolean literals as default parameter values that are not defined as shared constants or centralized in a single location in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions in an inheritance chain (parent and child contracts) implement overlapping security-critical validation logic (e.g., allowance checks, zero-address validation, balance checks) with identical or structurally similar sequences of require statements, conditionals, or mapping accesses, and there is no observable shared helper function, abstract validation method, or centralized implementation that consolidates this logic across the inheritance hierarchy, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in parent and child contracts within the same inheritance chain contain identical or structurally similar sequences of require statements, conditionals, or mapping accesses that validate security-critical conditions (e.g., allowance checks using _allowances[owner][spender] >= amount, zero-address checks using address != address(0)), and there is no observable shared internal function call, abstract function with override, or centralized validation logic that establishes a single authoritative implementation in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a constructor or initialization function contains multiple conditional branches (e.g., if/else if blocks) that assign the same set of security-relevant state variables (router addresses, fee exclusion mappings, access control flags) across different blockchain chain IDs, with near-identical assignment sequences differing only in the chain ID condition and assigned values, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A constructor or initialization function in the same contract contains two or more conditional branches that check different chain IDs (e.g., block.chainid == 1, block.chainid == 56) and within each branch assign the same set of security-relevant state variables (identified by identical variable names across branches) with different values, without observable shared assignment logic or centralized configuration mechanism.

lessons.lesson: When multiple internal view functions in the same contract check the same mapping or state variable with different boolean combinations (e.g., `isLpPair[sender] && !isLpPair[recipient]`, `!isLpPair[sender] && isLpPair[recipient]`, `!isLpPair[sender] && !isLpPair[recipient]`) to determine distinct security-relevant operational contexts (e.g., transaction types like buy, sell, transfer), and these determinations influence security-critical operations (fee calculations, access controls, or validation logic) without observable centralized abstraction or shared helper function that consolidates the boolean logic, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more internal view functions in the same contract contain conditional expressions that reference the same mapping or state variable with different boolean combinations (e.g., using &&, ||, ! operators in different patterns), return boolean values that classify distinct operational contexts, and these return values are used in security-critical operations visible in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple public functions in the same contract call the same internal helper functions with identical or semantically equivalent parameters for security-critical operations (e.g., role management, access control, state transitions), and these calling functions differ only in permission modifiers (e.g., onlyRole vs public), validation checks, or minor parameter handling, without observable contextual separation that would prevent inconsistent maintenance across all call paths, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more public functions in the same contract call the same internal function(s) with identical or semantically equivalent parameter values for security-critical operations, and the calling functions differ only in access control modifiers, additional require statements, or minor parameter validation logic visible in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple external functions in the same contract delegate to the same internal function for security-critical operations (access control, validation, or state transitions) with identical or semantically equivalent parameter patterns, and there is no observable synchronization mechanism (shared modifier, centralized validation, or single authoritative update path) that ensures consistent logic across all delegation paths in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more external functions in the same contract call the same internal function with identical or semantically equivalent parameter values for security-critical operations, and there is no observable shared modifier, centralized validation logic, or single authoritative update path that ensures all delegation paths remain synchronized.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions in the same contract contain identical or structurally similar access control modifiers (e.g., onlyRole(ROLE_NAME)) and validation checks (e.g., require statements or conditionals with identical logical conditions) for security-critical operations, differing only in error message strings or minor implementation details, and there is no observable shared modifier or validation function that centralizes this logic in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with risk of semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract have identical access control modifiers (e.g., onlyRole(LOCKER_ROLE)) and contain identical or structurally similar validation checks (e.g., if (_amount == 0) revert(...)) with the same logical condition, differing only in error message strings or minor implementation variations, without observable shared modifier or validation function calls that consolidate this logic.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions in the same contract contain identical or semantically equivalent security-critical validation checks (e.g., zero address checks, allowance validations, balance checks) with identical logical conditions and error messages, and these checks are not centralized into a shared helper function, abstract validation method, or single authoritative implementation visible in the source code, flag as vulnerable due to redundancy with concrete risk of semantic divergence during maintenance.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract contain identical require statements, conditionals, or modifier checks with identical logical conditions and error message strings that validate security-critical parameters, and there is no observable shared function call, inheritance relationship, or centralized validation logic that abstracts these checks in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When two or more functions call the same internal function (e.g., _approve) with arguments derived from the same storage variable (e.g., _allowances) and differ only in the presence of a require check or the use of unchecked arithmetic on the derivation, flag as vulnerable because this duplicated logic with minor discrepancies creates a maintenance risk of inconsistent security guarantees.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract call the same internal function with arguments derived from the same storage variable, and the functions differ only in the presence of a require statement or the use of unchecked arithmetic in the derivation of those arguments.

lessons.lesson: When two or more functions perform the same core security-sensitive operation (e.g., token transfer from msg.sender to multiple recipients) using similar iteration, require checks, and calls to the same internal _transfer function, even if the amount calculation differs (e.g., fixed amounts vs percentages), flag as vulnerable because the duplicated logic creates a risk of inconsistent updates and semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract iterate over arrays of addresses, call the same internal transfer function from msg.sender, include identical require statements for balance or allowance checks, and differ only in how the transfer amount is computed (e.g., fixed amount vs percentage of balance).

lessons.lesson: When a contract contains multiple functions that modify the same security-critical state variable through independent logical paths (e.g., a dedicated setter and a conditional assignment in a transfer function) and also contains private toggle functions that duplicate the effect of temporarily changing that state without synchronization, flag as vulnerable because the redundancy creates a concrete risk of semantic divergence and inconsistent application.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract assign values to the same security-critical state variable (e.g., _taxFee) through different logical paths (e.g., a dedicated setter function and a conditional assignment inside a transfer function), and there exists at least one additional function (e.g., a private toggle) that temporarily sets that variable to a fixed value (e.g., 0) without observable synchronization or shared implementation that ensures consistency across all modification paths.

lessons.lesson: When multiple type-specific wrapper functions for arithmetic operations contain additional scaling operations (division by different constants) that are duplicated across types, judge vulnerable because the redundant scaling logic may diverge and introduce inconsistencies.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more wrapper functions in the same contract or library call a common underlying function for the same core arithmetic operation, and each wrapper contains an additional scaling division by a type-specific constant (e.g., expScale, doubleScale) that is not present in the underlying function, and there is no observable shared helper function or centralized logic that abstracts the scaling.

lessons.lesson: When a contract defines multiple functions (e.g., add_, sub_, mul_, div_) for different struct types (e.g., Exp, Double, uint) that all perform the same core arithmetic operation with only scaling differences (e.g., division by expScale vs doubleScale), and the functions are not all funneled through a single authoritative implementation (i.e., each type has its own copy of the scaling logic), flag as vulnerable because the duplication creates a maintenance risk for future changes, even if all copies are currently consistent.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract or library perform the same core arithmetic operation (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) on different struct types, where each function contains an additional scaling division by a type-specific constant (e.g., expScale, doubleScale) that is not present in a shared underlying function, and there is no observable shared helper function or centralized logic that abstracts the scaling.

lessons.lesson: When identical initialization logic (i.e., the same conditional check and state assignment) appears verbatim in multiple functions without being abstracted into a shared helper, judge vulnerable because the duplication introduces a maintenance risk regardless of lifecycle context differences.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract contain identical sequences of conditional checks and state variable assignments that perform initialization of security-critical state (e.g., anti-snipe flags, fee exclusions, or access control settings), and there is no observable shared helper function, inheritance relationship, or centralized logic that consolidates this initialization logic in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When a contract defines parallel sets of fee variables for different contexts (e.g., regular vs sale) and includes a function that copies one set into the other without also updating the backup variables used in a restore function, flag as vulnerable because the restore function can reintroduce stale values, creating a latent inconsistency between the redundant code paths.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more sets of state variables store related security-critical values (e.g., fee rates) for different operational contexts, and there exists a function that copies values from one set to another, but does not update a separate set of backup variables that are used by a restore function to revert to previous values.

lessons.lesson: When a contract inherits an empty virtual hook function (e.g., _afterTokenTransfer) and does not override it, but the hook is called from multiple internal functions (e.g., _transfer, _mint, _burn), judge vulnerable to CWE-1041 because the repeated calls to an empty function are unnecessary duplicates that increase code surface without benefit.
lessons.applies_when: A contract inherits a virtual hook function that is empty (no statements) and does not override it, yet the hook is called from two or more distinct internal functions (e.g., _transfer, _mint, _burn) within the same contract.

lessons.lesson: When a contract contains two or more libraries with structurally similar arithmetic functions that differ only in signed/unsigned integer type but share the same algorithmic intent, judge vulnerable because the duplication creates a maintenance risk where one path could be updated without the other, leading to inconsistent arithmetic overflow/underflow prevention.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more libraries in the same contract or inheritance chain contain functions with identical names and parameter structures that perform arithmetic safety operations (add, sub, mul, div, mod), differing only in the declared integer type (e.g., uint256 vs int256), without observable type-specific logic variations, optimization patterns, or separate compilation directives that would justify independent maintenance.

lessons.lesson: When a contract contains two separate libraries that implement the same arithmetic operations (add, sub, mul, div) for different integer types (e.g., uint256 and int256) and the function bodies are structurally identical except for type-specific bounds checks inherent to the integer type, judge vulnerable because the duplication is unnecessary and creates a maintenance risk without a clear justification visible in the source code.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more libraries in the same contract or inheritance chain contain functions with identical names and parameter structures that perform arithmetic safety operations (add, sub, mul, div, mod), differing only in the declared integer type (e.g., uint256 vs int256), without observable type-specific logic variations, optimization patterns, or separate compilation directives that would justify independent maintenance.

lessons.lesson: When a function contains unreachable code (statements after an unconditional revert or require(false)) and that function is overridden in a derived contract, flag as vulnerable because the dead code is unnecessary and creates a redundant code path that complicates maintenance and risks future inconsistencies.
lessons.applies_when: A function in a parent contract contains one or more statements after an unconditional revert, require(false), or other unconditional control flow termination, and a child contract overrides that function with a different implementation, without observable removal or deactivation of the parent's dead code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions in a contract or library are overloaded wrappers that all call the same internal function with identical argument preparation and error handling, and differ only in how input parameters are parsed or encoded, judge vulnerable because the duplicated wrapper logic presents a maintenance risk if any wrapper is later modified independently.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract or library call the same internal function with identical argument preparation and error handling logic, and differ only in the parsing or encoding of input parameters (e.g., bytes memory signature vs separate v, r, s components).

lessons.lesson: When a contract imports a library that is not used in any function or state variable operation and another library with overlapping functionality (e.g., arithmetic overflow checks) is used, judge vulnerable because the unused library is redundant and can obscure the authoritative implementation.
lessons.applies_when: A contract imports a library that is not referenced in any function call, state variable type, or modifier within the contract, and another library with overlapping or identical functionality (e.g., both provide arithmetic safety functions) is imported and used.

lessons.lesson: When a library or contract defines multiple function overloads with the same operation name, and the shorter-parameter version's body consists entirely of a single call to the longer-parameter version with a constant default argument, and no other logic or access control differs, judge vulnerable because the redundant overloads do not serve distinct lifecycle or permission contexts and risk semantic divergence during maintenance.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more function overloads in the same library or contract have the same operation name, and the shorter-parameter version contains only a single call to the longer-parameter version with a hard-coded constant default argument (e.g., a string literal, numeric literal, or boolean literal), and there is no additional logic, access control, or parameter transformation in the shorter version.

lessons.lesson: When a contract defines internal functions that are only invoked by external privileged functions and their functionality is already enforced by modifiers on all state-changing functions, and the internal functions are not called within the core business logic, judge those internal functions as redundant because they are dead code that could cause maintenance divergence.
lessons.applies_when: A contract contains internal functions (e.g., _pause, _unpause) that are only called from external owner-only functions, and the same functionality (e.g., whenNotPaused modifier) is already applied to all state-changing functions via modifiers, and the internal functions are not called within any other business logic.

lessons.lesson: When a contract imports and uses a library (e.g., SafeMath) that duplicates arithmetic overflow/underflow protection already provided by the Solidity compiler version (0.8.0+), and the library functions are used in place of built-in operators, judge the library usage as redundant code that increases maintenance risk.
lessons.applies_when: A contract imports a library that provides arithmetic safety functions (e.g., add, sub, mul, div) and uses those functions in its code, while the contract is compiled with Solidity version 0.8.0 or later, which includes built-in overflow/underflow checks for standard arithmetic operators.

lessons.lesson: When multiple private functions in the same contract contain identical sequences of arithmetic operations and state updates on balance mappings (e.g., _rOwned, _tOwned), differing only in which mapping variable is used for each address, and these functions are not generated from a shared template or parameterized, judge vulnerable because the duplication creates a maintenance risk where a change to one function may not be propagated to others, potentially causing inconsistent accounting.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more private functions in the same contract contain identical sequences of arithmetic operations and state variable assignments that update different balance mappings (e.g., _rOwned and _tOwned) for the same addresses, and there is no observable shared function call, inheritance relationship, or centralized logic that consolidates these sequences in the source code.

lessons.lesson: When multiple functions in the same contract independently implement the same state variable update pattern (e.g., reading a mapping, performing a check, and writing back) with only arithmetic direction differences (e.g., addition vs subtraction), and no single internal function encapsulates that update for all such functions, flag as vulnerable because redundant code maintenance risks semantic divergence.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more functions in the same contract contain sequences that read a mapping, perform a require or conditional check, and write back to the same mapping, where the only difference is the arithmetic operation (e.g., addition vs subtraction) applied to the value, and there is no observable shared internal function that performs the update for all such functions.

lessons.lesson: When a function in a parent contract is overridden in a child contract to implement the same security policy (e.g., both revert) but with different code (different error message, modifier, or minor logic), and there is no mechanism enforcing synchronization between the copies, judge vulnerable because the redundancy creates a risk that future maintenance will miss updates to one copy, leading to inconsistent behavior and potential security gaps.
lessons.applies_when: A child contract overrides a virtual function from a parent contract, and both the parent and child implementations contain unconditional revert statements (or require(false)) that implement the same security policy (e.g., disabling transfers), but the implementations differ in error message strings, modifiers, or minor logic, and there is no observable shared function call, inheritance relationship, or centralized logic that ensures both implementations remain synchronized.

lessons.lesson: Treat duplicated guard/precondition blocks across multiple helper functions in the same “operation family” (e.g., call/staticcall/delegatecall helpers) as redundancy even when the core execution/revert-handling is centralized in a shared verifier; repeated `require(...)`/`if(...) revert` gatekeeping is itself a drift-prone duplicated security check.
lessons.applies_when: Two or more helper functions in the same contract/library each perform a low-level call variant (e.g., `.call`, `.staticcall`, `.delegatecall`) and each contains an identical or near-identical guard block such as `require(isContract(target), ...)`, `require(address(this).balance >= value, ...)`, `require(success, ...)`, or equivalent precondition checks, and those guards are not delegated to a single shared internal helper that all variants call before executing.

lessons.lesson: Treat “API-level duplication” as redundancy: an explicit getter function that only returns a storage variable is redundant when that variable is already `public` (thus has an auto-generated getter), because it creates two externally visible entrypoints for the same datum that can drift (e.g., later one adds access checks, formatting, or different return source).
lessons.applies_when: In a contract: (1) a state variable is declared `public` (any type), and (2) there exists a `public`/`external` function whose body is only `return <thatVariable>;` (optionally with trivial casts), and (3) the function name differs from the compiler getter name (i.e., it is not just the generated getter).

lessons.lesson: Flag wrapper getters that only delegate to another getter as redundant API surface (e.g., `getOwner() { return owner(); }`), even if the underlying logic is centralized, because the duplication is at the entrypoint level and can later diverge in semantics or authorization.
lessons.applies_when: Two `public`/`external` functions in the same contract or inheritance chain return the same value, and one function’s entire body is a single `return otherFunction(<same args or fixed args like msg.sender>);` with no additional statements (no validation, no state writes, no events).

lessons.lesson: Treat duplicated inlined precondition guards as redundancy even when the surrounding operations differ: repeating the same `require(...)`/`if (...) revert` invariant (especially zero-address checks) across multiple internal state-transition functions is itself redundant, drift-prone security logic if not centralized via a shared modifier/helper.
lessons.applies_when: Within the same contract/inheritance chain, two or more internal/private state-changing functions (e.g., `_transfer`, `_mint`, `_burn`, `_approve`, or similar) each contain an identical or near-identical guard checking a parameter against `address(0)` (e.g., `require(to != address(0), ...)`, `require(from != address(0), ...)`, `if (account == address(0)) revert ...;`), and there is no shared modifier or internal helper that all those functions call to perform that check.

lessons.lesson: Treat duplicated multi-step liquidity/LP-mint workflows as redundancy even when separated into “initial” vs “regular” helpers: if two functions each inline the same sequence of external token transfers followed by an LP mint, and differ only in how a key amount (typically WETH/ETH amount) is sourced (computed vs parameter), that is two authoritative implementations with drift risk and should be flagged.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract/library, there exist two or more functions that (a) perform an ordered workflow including at least: (1) token transfer/transferFrom (or IERC20.safeTransfer/safeTransferFrom) of one asset, (2) token transfer/transferFrom of a second asset (often WETH/ETH wrapper), and (3) a DEX pair interaction such as `pair.mint(...)` (or similar LP mint call), and (b) the functions’ bodies are structurally similar in these steps but differ mainly in whether one of the transferred amounts is derived internally (e.g., `amountWeth = msg.value` / computed from reserves) while the other function receives that amount as a parameter, and (c) neither function delegates the shared sequence to a single internal helper that contains the authoritative workflow.

lessons.lesson: Treat duplicated allowance-spend/update subsequences as redundancy even if the surrounding operation differs (e.g., transfer vs burn): repeating the exact pattern “compute new allowance from _allowances[owner][spender] via .sub(...) then persist via _approve(owner, spender, newAllowance)” across multiple functions without a shared helper is a drift-prone duplicate security check/update.
lessons.applies_when: Within the same contract/inheritance chain, two or more distinct functions each contain (1) a read of the allowance mapping (e.g., `_allowances[owner][spender]` or `allowance(owner, spender)`), (2) subtraction of `amount` (or equivalent) from that value (commonly `.sub(amount, <errMsg>)` or `- amount`), and (3) a subsequent `_approve(owner, spender, newAllowance)` call (or equivalent allowance write), and none of those functions delegate that 3-step allowance update to a single shared internal helper (e.g., `_spendAllowance(...)`).

lessons.lesson: Treat ERC20-style wrapper entrypoints as redundant code even when they only delegate: a `transfer(...)` implementation that is solely `return transferFrom(msg.sender, to, amount)` (or equivalent) is a second public API for the same operation and is drift-prone if either path later changes (fees, hooks, checks).
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract/inheritance chain: (1) a `public`/`external` function `transfer(address,uint256)` exists, and (2) its body contains no logic besides a single call/return to `transferFrom` (or similarly named function) where the first argument is `msg.sender` and the remaining arguments are the wrapper’s parameters, and (3) there are no additional statements (no requires, state writes, events) in the wrapper.

lessons.lesson: Treat payable fallback/receive functions that only forward to an explicit deposit/entry function as redundant entrypoints: `fallback()/receive()` that does nothing but call `deposit()` is a duplicated externally reachable path to the same state transition and should be flagged as redundant API surface.
lessons.applies_when: A contract defines `fallback() external payable` or `receive() external payable` whose body is only a single call (or `return` call) to another function in the same contract (commonly `deposit()`), with no other statements (no requires, state writes, events, parameter transforms).

lessons.lesson: Treat externally reachable delegation-only entrypoints as redundancy even when they centralize internal logic: a payable `receive()`/`fallback()` that only forwards to a deposit-like function is a duplicate public API path for the same state transition and should be flagged for CWE-1041.
lessons.applies_when: A contract defines `receive() external payable` or `fallback() external payable` and the function body consists solely of a single internal call to another function in the same contract (e.g., `deposit();` / `return deposit();`) with no other statements (no require/revert, no state writes, no events, no parameter transformation).

lessons.lesson: Do not require “full duplicated implementations” to flag CWE-1041: a repeated, security-critical accounting motif (a multi-statement subsequence) duplicated across distinct entrypoints is sufficient redundancy evidence when it could be centralized into one helper but is not.
lessons.applies_when: Within the same contract/inheritance chain, there exist two or more distinct state-changing functions (e.g., transferFrom, burnFrom, _burnFrom, permit-like spenders) that each inline the same allowance-spend subsequence: (1) read `_allowances[owner][spender]` (or `allowance(owner, spender)`), (2) compute `newAllowance` by subtracting `amount` (via `.sub(amount, <err>)`, `unchecked { ... - amount }` with a check, or `- amount` with a require), and (3) write it back via `_approve(owner, spender, newAllowance)` (or direct mapping write), and none of these functions delegates that subsequence to a single shared internal helper (e.g., `_spendAllowance`).

lessons.lesson: Count duplicated post-operation “side-effect” sequences as redundant code even when the core operation is centralized via `super`: if multiple token-operation entrypoints each repeat the same governance/accounting side-effect (e.g., `_moveDelegates(...)`, vote checkpoint writes) after calling `super._transfer/_mint/_burn`, that repeated side-effect is the redundant logic and should be flagged unless it is funneled through a single hook/helper.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract/inheritance chain: (1) there are 2+ distinct token operation functions (e.g., overrides of `_transfer`, `_mint`, `_burn`, or external `mint/burn/transfer/transferFrom` wrappers) that each call a `super.<op>(...)` (or an internal core op), and (2) in each function body there is an additional, structurally identical side-effect subsequence (same callee such as `_moveDelegates`/checkpoint updater, with arguments derived from the same inputs like `from/to/amount`), and (3) that side-effect is not centralized via a single shared internal helper (e.g., `_afterTokenTransfer` override) that all ops invoke.

lessons.lesson: Treat overload/forwarder chains as redundancy even when core logic is centralized: a public/internal overload whose entire body is a single call/return to another overload with one or more hard-coded default arguments (e.g., fixed revert string, fixed boolean, fixed numeric literal) is redundant code because it creates parallel, drift-prone API surfaces without adding distinct lifecycle/permission context.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract/library, there exist 2+ functions with the same name (or clearly same operation family) and different parameter lists, and at least one shorter-parameter overload has a body consisting solely of `return f(<same args>, <constant literal(s)>);` or `f(<same args>, <constant literal(s)>);` where the extra arguments are compile-time constants (string/bytes literal, numeric literal, boolean literal, address literal), with no other statements (no require/if, no state writes, no events).

lessons.lesson: Flag redundant state-restoration logic when the same state variable is restored to a prior value via two or more distinct restore mechanisms (e.g., a helper like `restoreAllFees()` and a direct assignment `_taxFee = _previousTaxFee`) within the same contract, because this creates multiple authoritative reset paths that can drift independently.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract/inheritance chain: (1) a state variable `x` is saved to a backup variable (e.g., `prevX = x` or `backupX = x`), and (2) there exist two or more syntactically distinct restore sites that set `x` back from that backup (e.g., `restoreX()` that assigns `x = prevX`, plus at least one direct assignment `x = prevX` elsewhere, or multiple direct restores in different branches), and (3) the restore sites are not all funneled through a single internal helper (i.e., some restores bypass the helper).

lessons.lesson: Treat “parallel policy entrypoints” as redundancy even if all state writes are centralized: multiple externally callable role-membership mutators (admin-grant/admin-revoke/self-renounce/internal-setup) operating on the same `mapping(bytes32 => mapping(address => bool))` (or equivalent) create redundant authoritative paths that can drift in guards/events/invariants over time.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract/inheritance chain, there are 2+ distinct functions that mutate the same role-membership storage (e.g., `roles[role].members[account] = true/false` directly or via a shared helper like `_grantRole/_revokeRole/_setupRole`), and at least two of those functions are externally callable (`public`/`external`) or otherwise represent distinct call contexts (e.g., `grantRole` guarded by `onlyRole(getRoleAdmin(role))` vs `renounceRole` guarded by `require(account == msg.sender)`), indicating multiple public API paths for the same underlying membership state transition.

lessons.lesson: Flag “parallel role-management entrypoints” as redundant units even when state updates are centralized: multiple grant/revoke/setup/renounce functions that each differ only by their wrapper-specific authorization precondition are drift-prone redundancy at the API/policy layer.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract/inheritance chain: (1) there are 2+ distinct role-management functions (names indicative of grant/revoke/setup/renounce) that each consist primarily of a single call to the same internal role-mutator (e.g., `_grantRole(role, account)` or `_revokeRole(role, account)`), and (2) at least two of these wrapper functions have different access-control or caller-identity guards (e.g., `onlyRole(getRoleAdmin(role))` vs `require(account == msg.sender)`), while sharing the same downstream helper for the actual storage mutation.

lessons.lesson: Treat “single vs batch” variants of the same state-changing operation as redundant units even when both delegate to the same internal helper: the batch loop plus per-item delegation is a parallel authoritative entrypoint that can drift in modifiers, per-item validation, event emission, or revert semantics.
lessons.applies_when: In the same contract/inheritance chain there exist two external/public functions where: (1) one accepts a single item (e.g., `address`/`uint256`/`bytes32`) and the other accepts an array of the same item type(s) (e.g., `address[]`/`uint256[]`), (2) both perform the same named operation family (same verb or same internal helper called), and (3) the batch function iterates over the array(s) and calls the same internal/state-changing helper (or calls the single-item function) per element, with no distinct lifecycle gating (e.g., no `initializer`/`onlyInitializing`) visible in the signatures/modifiers.
